flashcards.back: Its radius doubles, since the Schwarzschild radius is directly proportional to mass.

flashcards.front: What is a gravitational wave, and why is it hard to detect?
flashcards.back: A ripple in spacetime from accelerating masses; detection is hard because the distortions are far smaller than an atomic nucleus, needing instruments like LIGO.

flashcards.front: What makes a binary a good black-hole candidate?
flashcards.back: An unseen companion more massive than about 3 solar masses that also emits strong X-rays from an accretion disk.

flashcards.front: Why do we see the Milky Way as a band across the sky?
flashcards.back: We are inside its flat disk, so looking along the disk we see the merged light of countless distant stars as a hazy band.

flashcards.front: Where are globular clusters, molecular clouds, and atomic hydrogen found in a spiral galaxy?
flashcards.back: Globular clusters in the halo; molecular clouds and atomic hydrogen in the disk, concentrated in the spiral arms.

flashcards.front: How do Population I and II stars differ?
flashcards.back: Pop I are young, metal-rich disk stars on circular orbits; Pop II are old, metal-poor halo/bulge stars on random orbits.

flashcards.front: What are the main components of the Milky Way?
flashcards.back: A disk with spiral arms, a central bulge, a spherical halo with globular clusters, a dark-matter halo, and a central black hole.

flashcards.front: What is the evidence for a black hole at the Galaxy's center?
flashcards.back: Stars near the center orbit an unseen, compact object at high speed, implying about 4 million solar masses in a tiny region (Sagittarius A*).

flashcards.front: What is the long-term future of the Milky Way?
flashcards.back: Continued (slowing) star formation, followed by a collision and merger with the Andromeda galaxy in a few billion years.

flashcards.front: Describe the three main galaxy types.
flashcards.back: Spirals (disk, arms, gas, star formation), ellipticals (rounded, old stars, little gas), and irregulars (no regular shape, often gas-rich and star-forming).

flashcards.front: Why did it take so long to confirm other galaxies exist?
flashcards.back: They look like faint fuzzy nebulae; only Cepheid measurements in Andromeda (1920s) proved their distances lie far beyond the Milky Way.

flashcards.front: What is Hubble's law?
flashcards.back: A galaxy's recession velocity is proportional to its distance (v = H0 times d), the key evidence for an expanding universe.

flashcards.front: What does it mean that the universe is expanding?
flashcards.back: Space itself stretches, increasing distances between unbound galaxies; bound systems like the solar system and your room do not expand.

flashcards.front: Why is a spiral's disk dark when seen edge-on?
flashcards.back: Dust in the disk plane absorbs and blocks the light of stars behind it.

flashcards.front: What causes the highest mass-to-light ratios?
flashcards.back: Dark matter, which adds mass but emits no light.

flashcards.front: Two ways to measure distance to a distant spiral galaxy?
flashcards.back: Type Ia supernovae (luminous standard candles) and the Tully-Fisher relation.

flashcards.front: If all galaxies recede from us, are we at the center of the universe?
flashcards.back: No; in a uniform expansion every observer sees all others receding, so there is no center.

flashcards.front: What key observations must any cosmology explain?
flashcards.back: The expansion of the universe, the cosmic microwave background, the light-element abundances, and the large-scale uniformity and structure.

flashcards.front: What determines the future of the universe, and what is observed?
flashcards.back: Its total density and energy content (including dark energy); observations show the expansion is accelerating, so it will expand forever.

flashcards.front: Which formed first: hydrogen nuclei or hydrogen atoms?
flashcards.back: Nuclei formed first (in the first minutes); neutral atoms formed about 380,000 years later at recombination.

flashcards.front: What two observations strongly support the Big Bang?
flashcards.back: The cosmic microwave background radiation and the observed primordial abundances of light elements like helium.

flashcards.front: What two problems does inflation solve?
flashcards.back: The flatness problem and the horizon problem.

flashcards.front: Why do astronomers believe in dark matter, and what is dark energy?
flashcards.back: Galaxies rotate and clusters hold together with far more gravity than visible matter provides (dark matter); dark energy is the energy driving accelerating expansion.

flashcards.front: Why does all distant galaxy light show a redshift?
flashcards.back: Space itself expands and stretches the light's wavelength (cosmological redshift) as it travels.

flashcards.front: What is the evidence that cosmic expansion is accelerating?
flashcards.back: Distant Type Ia supernovae appear fainter (farther) than expected for a decelerating universe.

flashcards.front: What does the lecture say science must be above all else?
flashcards.back: Honest — willing to seek out the ways our current ideas might be wrong, which is how science self-corrects toward the truth.
flashcards.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How does the video distinguish astronomy from astrology?
flashcards.back: Astronomy is evidence-based science; astrology is not, because its 'successes' come from remembering hits and forgetting misses, not real predictive power.
flashcards.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Who counts as 'doing astronomy,' according to the video?
flashcards.back: Not only telescope operators, but astrophysicists, engineers, technicians, programmers, teachers, writers, and even artists.
flashcards.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Roughly how much of the universe can we directly see, per the video?
flashcards.back: Only about 4 percent.
flashcards.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Where did the elements in your body come from, per the video?
flashcards.back: From exploding stars, which forged the iron in your blood, calcium in your bones, and phosphorus in your DNA.
flashcards.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Why does the north star appear not to move?
flashcards.back: It lies almost directly above Earth's rotation axis, a point that experiences no lateral motion as Earth spins.
flashcards.source: Course Video 2: History of Astronomy I: The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: The video stresses that lunar phases are NOT caused by what?
flashcards.back: Earth's shadow; phases come from how much of the Moon's sunlit face is turned toward Earth.
flashcards.source: Course Video 2: History of Astronomy I: The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Why are different constellations visible at different times of year?
flashcards.back: As Earth orbits the Sun, the Sun blocks different background stars, so different ones appear in the night sky.
flashcards.source: Course Video 2: History of Astronomy I: The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What is the cause of the seasons, per the video?
flashcards.back: The 23.5-degree tilt of Earth's axis, which changes how directly sunlight strikes a hemisphere and the length of daylight — not Earth's distance from the Sun.
flashcards.source: Course Video 2: History of Astronomy I: The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How did Aristotle argue that Earth is round?
flashcards.back: From Earth's curved shadow on the Moon during lunar eclipses and from the way different stars become visible as you travel north or south.
flashcards.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How did Eratosthenes measure Earth's circumference?
flashcards.back: He compared the Sun's noon angle at Syene (overhead) and Alexandria (7 degrees off vertical); since 7 degrees is about 1/50 of a circle, the circumference is about 50 times the distance between the cities.
flashcards.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What value did Eratosthenes obtain for Earth's circumference?
flashcards.back: About 250,000 stadia, roughly 25,000 miles, close to the true value.
flashcards.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What did Aristarchus deduce about the Moon and the Sun?
flashcards.back: That the Moon's diameter is about one-third of Earth's, that the Sun is much larger than Earth, and that Earth might orbit the Sun.
flashcards.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How did Ptolemy's model explain retrograde motion?
flashcards.back: With epicycles: planets moved on small circles whose centers moved along their larger orbits around Earth.
flashcards.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How does the heliocentric model explain Mars's retrograde motion?
flashcards.back: Earth, on a faster inner orbit, overtakes the slower Mars, making Mars appear to move backward — like passing a slower car on the highway.
flashcards.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What was the valid objection to Copernicus involving parallax, and how was it resolved?
flashcards.back: If Earth moved, nearby stars should shift in position; the shift is real but so tiny (because stars are so distant) that it could not be measured until much later, when it confirmed heliocentrism.
flashcards.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How does parallax give a star's distance?
flashcards.back: Earth's positions six months apart provide a baseline of 1 AU; half the star's apparent shift is its parallax angle, and trigonometry then yields the distance.
flashcards.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What is the 'grid' or factor-label method of unit conversion?
flashcards.back: You multiply the starting quantity by conversion-factor fractions arranged so that the units you don't want cancel out, leaving only the units you want.
flashcards.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Using the video's method, what is 70 mph in km/s (with 1 mi = 1.6 km)?
flashcards.back: About 0.03 km/s, or 3 x 10^-2 km/s.
flashcards.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Using the video's method, what is 70 km/s in mph?
flashcards.back: About 156,586 mph, or roughly 1.57 x 10^5 mph.
flashcards.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How do you write 0.03 in scientific notation?
flashcards.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Why is scientific notation useful in astronomy?
flashcards.back: It compactly expresses extremely large and extremely small numbers without long strings of zeros, reducing errors.
flashcards.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What shape did Kepler find for planetary orbits, and what had been assumed before?
flashcards.back: Ellipses with the Sun at one focus; previously orbits were assumed to be perfect circles.
flashcards.source: Course Video 6: History of Astronomy IV: Kepler's Laws and Beyond (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Why do the planets' elliptical orbits look nearly circular?
flashcards.back: Their eccentricity is low, meaning the two foci are close together, so the ellipse is almost round.
flashcards.source: Course Video 6: History of Astronomy IV: Kepler's Laws and Beyond (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Which of Galileo's observations was the 'final nail' for geocentrism, per the video?
flashcards.back: The phases of Venus, which show that Venus orbits the Sun.
flashcards.source: Course Video 6: History of Astronomy IV: Kepler's Laws and Beyond (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What did Newton demonstrate about Kepler's laws?
flashcards.back: That they are consequences of his own laws of motion and universal gravitation, and that falling objects and orbiting planets obey the same physics.
flashcards.source: Course Video 6: History of Astronomy IV: Kepler's Laws and Beyond (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between mass and weight, per the video?
flashcards.back: Mass is the amount of stuff in an object (and its resistance to changes in motion) and is the same everywhere; weight is how hard a surface pushes back on it, which is zero in free fall.
flashcards.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: How does gravity change if you move 10 times farther from an object?
flashcards.back: The force drops to 1/100 of its value (10 x 10), by the inverse-square law.
flashcards.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: Why are astronauts on the ISS weightless even though gravity there is about 90 percent of surface value?
flashcards.back: Because they are in free fall, orbiting the Earth, so nothing pushes back on them; it is weightlessness from free fall, not an absence of gravity.
flashcards.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: List the five orbit shapes described in the video, in order of increasing speed.
flashcards.back: Line, circle, ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola.
flashcards.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: What are the approximate escape velocities for Earth, Jupiter, and the Sun?
flashcards.back: About 11 km/s for Earth, 58 km/s for Jupiter, and 600 km/s for the Sun.
flashcards.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

flashcards.front: When is a scientific theory proven correct?
flashcards.back: Never - data can support a conclusion, but nothing is ever proven (we don't know what we don't know).

flashcards.front: Format of scientific notation
flashcards.back: A number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of ten (e.g., 1,000,000,000,000 = 1 x 10^12).

flashcards.front: Sig fig rule: leading zeros
flashcards.back: Leading zeros are NEVER significant.

flashcards.front: Sig fig rule: trailing zeros
flashcards.back: Trailing zeros are significant ONLY if there is a decimal point somewhere in the number.

flashcards.front: Sig fig rule: sandwiched zeros
flashcards.back: Zeros between non-zero digits are ALWAYS significant.

flashcards.front: Sig figs when adding/subtracting
flashcards.back: Report to the same number of DECIMAL places as the value with the fewest (7.42 + 3.5 = 10.9).

flashcards.front: Sig figs when multiplying/dividing
flashcards.back: Report to the same number of SIG FIGS as the value with the fewest (100 x 53 = 5000; 100.0 x 53 = 5,300).

flashcards.front: How big and how old is the Sun?
flashcards.back: ~100x Earth's diameter, ~300x more massive, ~5 billion years old (about halfway through its life).

flashcards.front: How does the Sun generate energy?
flashcards.back: By converting (fusing) hydrogen into helium.

flashcards.front: How far and how big is the Moon?
flashcards.back: ~250,000 miles away, ~1/4 Earth's diameter, ~1/80 of Earth's mass; an airless, pitted ball of rock.

flashcards.front: What is the nearest star to the Sun and how far?
flashcards.back: Proxima Centauri, about 4.2 ly (~25 trillion miles) away.

flashcards.front: What cluster is the Milky Way part of?
flashcards.back: The Local Group - a small cluster of several dozen galaxies, a few million ly across.

flashcards.front: Roughly how old is the visible universe?
flashcards.back: About 13.8 billion years.

flashcards.front: What is dark matter?
flashcards.back: Matter that emits no observed radiation; deduced to outweigh luminous matter ~5:1; may interact only via gravity and the weak force.

flashcards.front: What is dark energy?
flashcards.back: The energy thought to drive the accelerating expansion of the universe.

flashcards.front: Composition of the universe (approx.)
flashcards.back: ~69% dark energy, ~25% dark matter, ~5% ordinary (atomic) matter.

flashcards.front: What is the celestial sphere, and why is it useful?
flashcards.back: An imaginary sphere centered on Earth on which all stars appear equally distant. It's a model that's useful for mapping the sky, even though it doesn't match physical reality.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Horizon vs. zenith
flashcards.back: Horizon = where the sky meets the ground. Zenith = the point directly overhead.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: In which directions do stars rise and set?
flashcards.back: They rise in the east and set in the west.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Why does the sky appear to turn each day?
flashcards.back: The celestial sphere stays still - Earth rotates. The apparent turning is an illusion caused by Earth's rotation.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What are the celestial poles, and what marks the north one?
flashcards.back: Points where Earth's extended axis meets the celestial sphere. Polaris (the North Star) sits very near the north celestial pole.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How does the altitude of the north celestial pole relate to your latitude?
flashcards.back: The pole's angle above the horizon equals the observer's latitude.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How does the sky's motion differ at the pole, equator, and mid-latitudes?
flashcards.back: At a pole: stars circle a fixed point and never rise/set. At the equator: stars rise straight up and set straight down. At mid-latitudes: stars rise/set at an angle; some are circumpolar.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Right ascension vs. declination
flashcards.back: RA is like longitude (east/west of the vernal equinox). Dec is like latitude (angle above/below the celestial equator).
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Modern definition of a constellation
flashcards.back: A defined patch (sector) of sky that carries a name - not just the set of stars. There are 88 official constellations (IAU, 1928); 48 are ancient.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Constellation vs. asterism
flashcards.back: A constellation is an official region of sky; an asterism is a recognizable star pattern within (or across) constellations, like the Big Dipper or Orion's Belt.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Name Orion's two famous stars.
flashcards.back: Betelgeuse (bright red, the armpit/shoulder) and Rigel (bright blue, the foot).
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Why do constellations look the same for thousands of years?
flashcards.back: Star positions change very slowly, so the patterns appear fixed over human timescales.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What is the zodiac?
flashcards.back: The belt of sky around the ecliptic, passing through ~12 constellations (plus Ophiuchus), through which the Sun, Moon, and planets travel.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Why are planets called 'planets'?
flashcards.back: It's Greek for 'wanderers' - unlike stars, they drift relative to the background stars.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What is retrograde motion, and is it real?
flashcards.back: The occasional apparent east-to-west drift of a planet against the stars. It's apparent only - caused by Earth and the planet passing each other, not a real reversal of orbit.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Why was retrograde motion historically important?
flashcards.back: Explaining it was a main reason astronomers ultimately rejected the Earth-centered model.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Eudoxus propose (~400-347 BC)?
flashcards.back: A geocentric model with each celestial object on its own revolving transparent sphere; faster-moving objects had smaller spheres.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How did Ptolemy explain retrograde motion?
flashcards.back: With epicycles - each planet moves on a small circle whose center moves on a larger circle around Earth. His model predicted motions with fair precision and was canon for centuries.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What is Occam's razor?
flashcards.back: Don't make more assumptions than necessary - 'entities should not be multiplied without necessity.' It helped topple the over-complicated geocentric models.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did the Mayans contribute (~750 BC)?
flashcards.back: They tracked the skies and created the first almanacs - tables predicting the movements of the Sun, Moon, and planets.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Thales of Miletus do in 585 BC?
flashcards.back: He correctly predicted a solar eclipse.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Pythagoras teach (~500 BC)?
flashcards.back: That Earth is round, based on the belief that the sphere is the perfect shape.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did the Babylonians devise (~400 BC)?
flashcards.back: The zodiac system, dividing the sky into 12 equal segments, improving how data was recorded and shared.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What three observations did Aristotle use for a round Earth?
flashcards.back: Earth's curved shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse; new stars appearing as you travel south; ships' hulls vanishing before their sail tops.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Aristarchus propose, and why was it rejected?
flashcards.back: A heliocentric model (Sun at center), after estimating the Sun's large size. It was rejected because no stellar parallax could be observed.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Eratosthenes measure (276-195 BC)?
flashcards.back: Earth's circumference - about 25,000 miles, close to the modern value - using shadow lengths at Alexandria vs. Syene on the summer solstice.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Name key Islamic contributions to astronomy.
flashcards.back: Preserving/expanding ancient texts, the astrolabe, much surviving vocabulary (zenith, Betelgeuse), questioning Ptolemy, and developing algebra and Arabic numerals.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Copernicus publish in 1543, and what did it argue?
flashcards.back: 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres,' presenting a heliocentric universe with Earth rotating on its axis; it explained retrograde motion as planets passing one another.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What were the limitations of Copernicus's model?
flashcards.back: It still used circular orbits, couldn't predict positions better than Ptolemy, couldn't explain the lack of stellar parallax, and clashed with Aristotelian 'common sense.'
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What was Tycho Brahe's main contribution?
flashcards.back: Extremely accurate naked-eye instruments and meticulous planetary data; he saw a supernova and comet (heavens are changeable) but, observing no parallax, proposed a compromise geocentric model.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How did Kepler improve the heliocentric model?
flashcards.back: Using Tycho's data, he showed planets orbit in ellipses (not circles) with the Sun at one focus, and formulated three laws of planetary motion.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: State Kepler's three laws.
flashcards.back: 1) Orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus. 2) A planet sweeps equal areas in equal times (faster when nearer the Sun). 3) Period squared is proportional to semimajor axis cubed.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What is eccentricity?
flashcards.back: A measure of how stretched an ellipse is; the closer to 1, the flatter (more elongated) the orbit.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Galileo observe with his telescope?
flashcards.back: Sunspots, lunar craters, four moons orbiting Jupiter, and that the Milky Way is countless stars - evidence against a simple Earth-centered universe.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: Why were Jupiter's moons such an important discovery?
flashcards.back: They are objects that orbit something other than Earth, directly contradicting the geocentric model.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What happened to Galileo in 1633?
flashcards.back: The Roman Inquisition found him 'vehemently suspect of heresy' for supporting heliocentrism; he was kept under house arrest until his death in 1642.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did Newton publish in 1687, and why did it matter?
flashcards.back: His three laws of motion and the theory of gravity, showing the force that makes an apple fall is the same one that holds the Moon in orbit. He also developed calculus.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: What did William Herschel discover in 1781?
flashcards.back: Uranus (and that stars can have companions). Irregularities in Uranus's orbit later led to the discovery of Neptune.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How did spectroscopy and Hubble change astronomy?
flashcards.back: Spectroscopy let astronomers determine stars' composition, and Edwin Hubble confirmed that galaxies exist beyond our own Milky Way.
flashcards.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

flashcards.front: How is a distance computed under Bode's Law?
flashcards.back: Take the sequence 0, 3, 6, 12, 24, ... (each term after 3 doubles), add 4 to the value, then divide by 10. The result is the predicted distance from the Sun in AU.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: Where does Bode's Law work well and where does it fail?
flashcards.back: It fits reasonably well from Mercury out to Uranus, but it breaks down for Neptune and Pluto and does not reliably apply to many exoplanetary systems.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: What is the formula for surface gravity, and what does it depend on?
flashcards.back: g = GM/R^2. It depends on the body's mass M and radius R: for a fixed radius, more mass means stronger surface gravity.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: What is Earth's surface gravity?
flashcards.back: About 9.8 m/s^2.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: What is the value of the gravitational constant G, and who measured it?
flashcards.back: About 6.67 x 10^-11 N·m^2/kg^2; Henry Cavendish measured it in 1798 using a torsion balance.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: Why is G hard to measure?
flashcards.back: Because gravity is extremely weak between laboratory-sized masses, so the attractive force to detect is tiny.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: What altitude marks a geosynchronous orbit, and what is special about it?
flashcards.back: About 35,786 km; a satellite there completes one orbit in exactly one day, matching Earth's rotation.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: How does a geostationary orbit differ from a geosynchronous one?
flashcards.back: A geostationary orbit is a geosynchronous orbit positioned over the equator, so the satellite stays above the same point on Earth at all times.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: Name the three altitude-based orbit classes and a use for each.
flashcards.back: Low Earth Orbit (<2000 km): ISS, Hubble, observation. Medium Earth Orbit (2000–35,786 km): navigation/communication/science. High Earth Orbit (>35,786 km): studying space, e.g., the Sun and solar wind.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: How do astronomers find a planet's average density?
flashcards.back: Divide mass by volume (rho = M/V): mass from Kepler's modified third law via a satellite's orbit, volume from the planet's measured radius.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: How is a large object's mass found from a smaller object's orbit?
flashcards.back: Using Kepler's modified third law, M = 4*pi^2*d^3 / (G*P^2), where d is the orbital distance and P the orbital period of the smaller body.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

flashcards.front: What two methods reveal a planet's composition besides density?
flashcards.back: Its reflection spectrum (atmosphere and surface rocks) and, for Earth specifically, seismic activity for interior structure.
flashcards.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: A light-year is a measure of which quantity?
questions.answer: Distance

questions.text: Approximately how old is the universe?
questions.answer: About 13.8 billion years

questions.text: Because light travels at a finite speed, observing a distant galaxy shows it as it was in the past.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: In science, the word 'theory' means little more than a casual guess.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The average distance between Earth and the Sun is called one astronomical ______.
questions.answer: unit

questions.text: The galaxy that contains our solar system is the ______ Way.
questions.answer: Milky

questions.text: Explain why astronomers say that telescopes are, in effect, time machines.
questions.answer: Light takes time to reach us, so the farther away an object is, the longer ago its light left it; observing distant objects therefore shows them as they were in the past, not as they are now.

questions.text: Describe the basic steps of the scientific method.
questions.answer: Make observations, propose a testable hypothesis, make predictions, test them with further observation or experiment, and revise or discard the hypothesis based on the evidence; well-supported explanations may become theories.

questions.text: Match each scale or object to the correct description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: The Sun plus everything bound to it
Billions of stars bound by gravity
The origin event of the universe

questions.text: Which list is correctly ordered from smallest to largest in physical scale?
questions.answer: Star, solar system, galaxy, universe

questions.text: The Milky Way contains roughly a few hundred billion stars.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The apparent backward motion of a planet against the background stars is called:
questions.answer: Retrograde motion

questions.text: Who is credited with reviving the heliocentric (Sun-centered) model in the 1500s?
questions.answer: Copernicus

questions.text: The daily rising and setting of stars is caused by Earth's rotation on its axis.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: In the magnitude system, brighter stars have larger positive numbers.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The Sun's apparent yearly path among the stars is called the ______.
questions.answer: ecliptic

questions.text: Ptolemy's geocentric model used small circles called ______ to reproduce retrograde motion.
questions.answer: epicycles

questions.text: Explain how the heliocentric model accounts for the retrograde motion of planets like Mars.
questions.answer: As Earth, on a faster inner orbit, overtakes a slower outer planet like Mars, the outer planet appears to drift backward against the distant stars for a time before resuming its normal eastward motion; it is an effect of the changing line of sight, not real backward motion.

questions.text: Why did the geocentric model persist for so long despite being incorrect?
questions.answer: It matched everyday experience (Earth feels stationary), agreed with the authority of Aristotle and Ptolemy, fit religious/philosophical views of the time, and—with enough epicycles—could predict planetary positions reasonably well.

questions.text: Match each term to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Sun's yearly path among the stars
Point the sky appears to rotate around

questions.text: Earth's slow ~26,000-year axial wobble is called:
questions.answer: Precession

questions.text: There are 88 official modern constellations covering the whole sky.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: According to Kepler's first law, the orbit of a planet is:
questions.answer: An ellipse with the Sun at one focus
options: An ellipse with the Sun at one focus

questions.text: Kepler's third law is best written as:
A planet moves fastest in its orbit when it is at perihelion (closest to the Sun).
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance between two objects.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The point in an orbit closest to the Sun is called ______.
questions.answer: perihelion

questions.text: Newton's second law of motion is written as the equation F = m______.
questions.answer: a

questions.text: Using Kepler's third law, explain how you would find the orbital period of a planet whose semimajor axis is 4 AU.
questions.answer: Kepler's third law gives P^2 = a^3. With a = 4 AU, a^3 = 64, so P^2 = 64 and P = 8 years.

questions.text: Describe how gravity keeps a planet in orbit instead of flying off in a straight line.
questions.answer: By inertia (Newton's first law) a planet tends to move in a straight line, but the Sun's gravity continuously pulls it toward the Sun. The constant sideways pull bends the straight-line path into a closed, curved orbit; the planet is essentially 'falling' around the Sun.

questions.text: Match each law to its statement.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Orbits are ellipses with the Sun at a focus
Equal areas swept in equal times

questions.text: The inverse-square nature of gravity means that if the distance between two masses doubles, the gravitational force becomes:
questions.answer: One-fourth as strong

questions.text: Newton showed that Kepler's three laws follow from his laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Earth's seasons are caused primarily by:
questions.answer: The tilt of Earth's rotation axis
options: Earth's changing distance from the Sun
The tilt of Earth's rotation axis

questions.text: We always see the same side of the Moon because the Moon:
questions.answer: Rotates once per orbit (synchronous rotation)
options: Rotates much faster than it orbits
Is tidally pushed away from Earth

questions.text: During a total solar eclipse, the Moon is positioned between the Sun and Earth.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Earth's seasons occur because Earth is much closer to the Sun in summer.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The two days each year when day and night are nearly equal in length are called the ______.
questions.answer: equinoxes

questions.text: Earth's rotation axis is tilted about 23.5 ______ from the perpendicular to its orbit.
questions.answer: degrees

questions.text: Explain why the tilt of Earth's axis, not its distance from the Sun, drives the seasons.
questions.answer: The tilt means each hemisphere is angled toward the Sun for part of the year (more direct sunlight and longer days = summer) and away for another part (less direct light, shorter days = winter). When one hemisphere has summer the other has winter, which couldn't happen if distance were the cause; in fact Earth is slightly closer to the Sun during Northern Hemisphere winter.

questions.text: Why don't solar and lunar eclipses happen every month?
questions.answer: The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees relative to Earth's orbit around the Sun, so most months the Moon passes above or below the exact Sun-Earth line. Eclipses only occur when the alignment is close enough during new moon (solar) or full moon (lunar).

questions.text: Match each event to its Sun-Earth-Moon geometry.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Moon opposite the Sun, fully lit face toward us
Moon between us and the Sun, dark face toward us

questions.text: A sidereal day (relative to the stars) is about:
questions.answer: 23 hours 56 minutes

questions.text: Rotation produces day and night, while revolution around the Sun produces the year.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Which of the following has the longest wavelength?
questions.answer: Radio waves

questions.text: A hot, low-density gas produces which kind of spectrum?
questions.answer: Emission-line spectrum

questions.text: All forms of electromagnetic radiation travel at the same speed in a vacuum.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: A blueshift in a spectrum means the object is moving away from us.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The change in observed wavelength caused by a source's motion toward or away from us is called the ______ effect.
questions.answer: Doppler

questions.text: According to Wien's law, the ______ an object is, the shorter the wavelength at which it radiates most strongly.
questions.answer: hotter

questions.text: Explain how astronomers can determine a star's temperature from its light.
questions.answer: A star radiates approximately as a blackbody, so the wavelength at which it is brightest depends on its temperature (Wien's law). Measuring the peak (or overall color) reveals the temperature: bluer peaks mean hotter stars, redder peaks mean cooler ones.

questions.text: Describe how the three types of spectra (continuous, emission, absorption) are produced.
questions.answer: A hot dense object gives a continuous spectrum (all wavelengths). A hot thin gas gives an emission-line spectrum (bright lines at specific wavelengths). A continuous source viewed through a cooler thin gas gives an absorption-line spectrum (dark lines where the gas removes specific wavelengths). These are summarized by Kirchhoff's laws.

questions.text: Match each spectrum or law to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Produced by a hot, dense object
Produced by a hot, thin gas
Dark lines from a cool gas in front of a hot source
Hotter objects peak at shorter wavelengths

questions.text: If a star's spectral lines are shifted toward longer (redder) wavelengths, the star is:
questions.answer: Moving away from us

questions.text: An emission-line spectrum can reveal which chemical elements are present in a glowing gas.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The single most important property of a telescope is its:
questions.answer: Aperture (diameter)

questions.text: If you double the diameter of a telescope's mirror, its light-gathering power increases by a factor of:
Reflecting telescopes use a curved mirror to gather and focus light.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Refracting telescopes are free of chromatic aberration.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The blurring of images caused by turbulence in Earth's atmosphere is called atmospheric ______.
questions.answer: seeing

questions.text: An electronic detector called a ______ has replaced photographic plates for recording astronomical images.
questions.answer: CCD

questions.text: Explain two distinct advantages of putting a telescope in space rather than on the ground.
questions.answer: First, it escapes atmospheric turbulence (seeing), allowing much sharper images. Second, it can observe wavelengths the atmosphere absorbs—such as ultraviolet, X-rays, and much of the infrared—that never reach the ground.

questions.text: Why do astronomers prefer building larger telescopes, and what two benefits does a larger aperture provide?
questions.answer: A larger aperture collects more light (light-gathering power scales with diameter squared), letting astronomers see fainter objects, and it provides finer resolution, letting them distinguish more detail and separate closely spaced objects.

questions.text: Match each instrument or technique to its purpose.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Uses a mirror to focus light

questions.text: Chromatic aberration is a problem associated specifically with:
questions.answer: Lenses

questions.text: Radio telescopes must generally be much larger than visible-light telescopes to achieve comparable resolution.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Define "Astronomy" and "Astrology."
questions.answer: Astronomy is the scientific study of celestial objects (stars, planets, galaxies, and so on), the universe as a whole, and the physical processes that govern them, based on observation and evidence. Astrology is the non-scientific belief that the positions of the Sun, Moon, and planets influence human affairs and personalities; it is not a science, makes no testable, reliably confirmed predictions, and should not be confused with astronomy.

questions.text: Determine the shape and size of Earth.
questions.answer: Earth is very nearly a sphere (slightly flattened at the poles). Its shape is shown by ships disappearing hull-first over the horizon, Earth's round shadow on the Moon during lunar eclipses, and the way visible constellations change with latitude. Its size was first estimated by Eratosthenes, who compared the Sun's noon angle at two cities a known distance apart and computed a circumference close to the true value of about 40,000 km (radius about 6,371 km).

questions.text: Discuss how the Scientific Method is used to solve problems.
questions.answer: The scientific method solves problems by insisting on testable, evidence-based explanations. One observes a phenomenon, forms a hypothesis, predicts what should follow if the hypothesis is true, tests it through observation or experiment, and then confirms, refines, or rejects it. Repeating this cycle weeds out wrong ideas, and explanations that survive extensive testing become accepted theories.

questions.text: Discuss the concept of "distances" and the importance of units.
questions.answer: Astronomical distances span an enormous range, so choosing the right unit is essential to keep numbers manageable and meaningful. Within the solar system, distances are given in astronomical units (AU, the Earth-Sun distance); between stars and galaxies, in light-years or parsecs. Besides convenience, these units convey scale and, for light-years, the look-back time, since light from a distant object left it long ago.

questions.text: Define the main features of the celestial sphere.
questions.answer: The celestial sphere is an imaginary sphere surrounding Earth onto which the stars appear projected. Its key features are the celestial poles (above Earth's poles, the points the sky seems to rotate around), the celestial equator (above Earth's equator), the ecliptic (the Sun's yearly path, tilted 23.5 degrees to the equator), the zenith (the point overhead), the horizon, and the meridian (the north-south line through the zenith).

questions.text: Describe how the motions of the Sun, Moon, stars, and planets appear to us on Earth.
questions.answer: All sky objects rise in the east and set in the west each day because of Earth's rotation. Over a year the Sun drifts eastward through the zodiac along the ecliptic (from Earth's orbit), while the Moon moves eastward and cycles through its phases about once a month. The planets generally drift eastward against the stars but occasionally appear to reverse into retrograde motion.

questions.text: Describe Ptolemy's geocentric system of planetary motion.
questions.answer: In Ptolemy's geocentric system, a stationary Earth sits at the center while the Sun, Moon, planets, and stars revolve around it. To reproduce the observed motions, including retrograde loops and varying speeds, Ptolemy had each planet move on a small circle (epicycle) whose center moved along a larger circle (deferent), with an offset reference point (the equant) added to improve the fit.

questions.text: Explain how Copernicus developed the heliocentric model of the solar system.
questions.answer: Dissatisfied with the complexity of the Ptolemaic system, Copernicus proposed placing the Sun, not Earth, at the center, with Earth and the other planets orbiting it and Earth also rotating daily. This Sun-centered arrangement naturally explained retrograde motion (as Earth overtakes outer planets) and gave a sensible ordering and spacing of the planets, offering a simpler overall picture.

questions.text: Describe Galileo's discoveries concerning the study of motion and forces.
questions.answer: Galileo established key ideas about motion: that objects of different mass fall at the same rate, and that a moving object continues moving unless a force acts on it (the principle of inertia), overturning the old belief that motion needs a constant push. His telescopic discoveries, the moons of Jupiter, the phases of Venus, lunar craters, and sunspots, provided evidence for the heliocentric model and laid groundwork that Newton later built upon.

questions.text: Describe how Tycho Brahe and Johannes Kepler contributed to our understanding of how planets move around the Sun.
questions.answer: Tycho Brahe made decades of remarkably precise naked-eye measurements of planetary positions, the best of the pre-telescope era. Kepler inherited that data and analyzed it, especially for Mars, discovering that the orbits are ellipses, not circles, and formulating his three laws of planetary motion. Brahe supplied the precision and Kepler supplied the mathematical insight.

questions.text: Explain Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.
questions.answer: First law: each planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. Second law: a line from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times, so a planet moves faster when nearer the Sun. Third law: the square of a planet's orbital period equals the cube of its semimajor axis (P^2 = a^3, with P in years and a in AU).

questions.text: Describe Newton's three laws of motion.
questions.answer: First law: an object stays at rest or in uniform straight-line motion unless acted on by a net force (inertia). Second law: a net force produces an acceleration equal to the force divided by the mass (F = ma). Third law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction, so forces always occur in pairs.

questions.text: Describe how Newton's universal law of gravitation extends our understanding of Kepler's laws.
questions.answer: Newton's law of gravitation states that every mass attracts every other with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. By combining this with his laws of motion, Newton showed that Kepler's three laws are not just empirical patterns but necessary consequences of gravity, and he generalized them, explaining why orbits are conic sections and adding the masses of the bodies into a more complete form of the third law.

questions.text: Compare the orbital characteristics of the planets in the solar system.
questions.answer: The planets all orbit the Sun in the same direction, in nearly the same plane, and in roughly circular orbits. Inner planets are closer, move faster, and have shorter periods, while outer planets are farther, move slower, and have longer periods (Neptune has the largest orbit and longest period). Mercury has the most eccentric and most inclined orbit of the eight, while most planets follow nearly circular paths.

questions.text: Explain how an object (such as a satellite) can be put into orbit around Earth.
questions.answer: A satellite stays in orbit by moving sideways fast enough that, as gravity pulls it down, its falling path curves around Earth at the same rate the surface curves away, so it continually 'falls around' the planet without hitting it. Reaching this requires accelerating it to orbital velocity (about 8 km/s for low Earth orbit) at sufficient altitude, which is what launch rockets provide.

questions.text: Explain how an object (such as a planetary probe) can escape from orbit.
questions.answer: To leave Earth permanently, a probe must reach escape velocity (about 11 km/s from Earth's surface), the speed at which its kinetic energy exceeds the energy binding it to Earth's gravity. At or above that speed it can coast away without falling back, allowing it to travel to other planets.

questions.text: Explain how the planet Neptune was discovered.
questions.answer: Astronomers noticed that Uranus was not following its predicted orbit, suggesting an unseen body was tugging on it. Using Newton's law of gravitation, Le Verrier and Adams independently calculated where such a planet should be, and in 1846 Neptune was found very close to the predicted position, which is why it is called the planet discovered 'with mathematics.'

questions.text: A comet's tail always points:
questions.answer: Away from the Sun
options: In the direction the comet is moving

questions.text: Most asteroids are found in a belt located between the orbits of:
questions.answer: Mars and Jupiter

questions.text: A meteorite is a fragment from space that survives its fall and reaches the ground.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Long-period comets originate in the Kuiper Belt just beyond Neptune.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The glowing cloud of gas and dust around a comet's nucleus is called the ______.
questions.answer: coma

questions.text: An asteroid or comet whose orbit brings it close to Earth is called a near-Earth ______.
questions.answer: object

questions.text: Explain why a comet develops a coma and tails as it approaches the Sun.
questions.answer: As a comet nears the Sun, increasing heat causes its ices to sublimate directly into gas, releasing trapped dust and forming the bright coma around the nucleus. Sunlight and the solar wind then push this gas and dust outward, creating an ion tail (driven straight back by the solar wind) and a curved dust tail, both pointing away from the Sun.

questions.text: Describe the relationship between comets and meteor showers.
questions.answer: Comets shed dust and debris along their orbital paths as they travel around the Sun. When Earth's orbit crosses one of these debris streams, the particles enter the atmosphere and burn up together, producing a meteor shower that recurs at the same date each year as Earth passes through the same stream.

questions.text: Match each object or feature to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Gas pushed straight back by the solar wind

questions.text: The brief streak of light from a particle burning up in the atmosphere is a:
questions.answer: Meteor

questions.text: Comets are composed largely of ices, while asteroids are largely rock and metal.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The Sun and planets formed from a rotating cloud of gas and dust called the:
questions.answer: solar nebula

questions.text: Radioactive dating of a rock tells us:
questions.answer: The time since that rock last solidified
options: The age of the entire planet
The time since that rock last solidified

questions.text: The frost line marks where it was cold enough in the nebula for ices to condense.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Hot Jupiters are common in our own solar system.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: Small solid bodies that combined to build the planets are called ______.
questions.answer: planetesimals

questions.text: A planet orbiting a star other than the Sun is called an ______.
questions.answer: exoplanet

questions.text: Outline the sequence of events in the solar nebula that produced planetesimals.
questions.answer: As the rotating solar nebula cooled, gases condensed into tiny solid grains. These grains collided and stuck together, gradually building up through accretion into larger clumps and then kilometer-sized planetesimals. The planetesimals in turn collided and merged to form protoplanets, which grew into the planets.

questions.text: Explain why dating a single rock does not necessarily give the age of the whole world it came from.
questions.answer: Radioactive dating measures the time since a particular rock solidified, not when the planet first formed. A surface freshly resurfaced by lava yields a young age even on an ancient world, so individual rocks can be much younger than the body; the oldest rocks found set a minimum age for the world as a whole.

questions.text: Which detection method finds an exoplanet by the periodic dimming of its star?
questions.answer: Transit method

questions.text: Primitive meteorites preserve material that has changed little since the solar system formed.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The visible surface of the Sun is called the:
questions.answer: photosphere

questions.text: Sunspots appear dark because they are:
questions.answer: cooler than their surroundings

questions.text: The Sun's corona is far hotter than its visible surface.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The sunspot cycle lasts about 100 years.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The continuous stream of charged particles flowing from the Sun is the solar ______.
questions.answer: wind

questions.text: A sudden, intense release of magnetic energy on the Sun is a solar ______.
questions.answer: flare

questions.text: Describe how energy travels from the Sun's core to its surface, naming the layers it passes through.
questions.answer: Energy generated by fusion in the core first moves outward through the radiative zone as photons that are absorbed and re-emitted countless times over a very long period. It then enters the convection zone, where hot gas physically rises and cooler gas sinks, carrying the energy upward. Finally it escapes as light from the photosphere, the visible surface.

questions.text: Explain the difference between the solar wind and a coronal mass ejection as causes of space weather.
questions.answer: The solar wind is a continuous, relatively gentle outflow of charged particles from the corona. A coronal mass ejection is a sudden, violent eruption that flings a huge cloud of plasma into space. The solar wind is steady, while CMEs are sporadic and far more intense, capable of triggering strong geomagnetic storms when they strike Earth.

questions.text: Match each solar feature to its description.
questions.answer: Match

questions.text: The Sun's full magnetic cycle, including a polarity reversal and return, lasts about:
questions.answer: 22 years

questions.text: Coronal mass ejections can disrupt satellites and power grids on Earth.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The Sun produces energy by:
questions.answer: nuclear fusion

questions.text: The net result of the proton-proton chain is to convert hydrogen into:
questions.answer: helium

questions.text: Neutrinos escape the Sun's core almost immediately, while light takes thousands of years to leak out.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Gravitational contraction could power the Sun for its full 4.6-billion-year lifetime.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The balance between gravity and outward pressure that keeps the Sun stable is called hydrostatic ______.
questions.answer: equilibrium

questions.text: Einstein's equation relating mass to energy is E = mc______.
Explain how we know the Sun's energy must come from nuclear fusion rather than chemical burning or contraction.
questions.answer: Chemical reactions could power the Sun for only a few thousand years and gravitational contraction for only tens of millions of years, yet rocks and meteorites show the Sun has shone steadily for about 4.6 billion years. Only nuclear fusion, which converts mass to energy via E = mc^2, releases enough energy to sustain that output for billions of years.

questions.text: Describe the proton-proton chain and its overall outcome.
questions.answer: In the first step two protons fuse into a deuterium nucleus, releasing a positron and a neutrino. In the second, deuterium fuses with another proton to form helium-3 and a gamma ray. In the third, two helium-3 nuclei combine to make helium-4 and release two protons. Overall, four hydrogen nuclei are converted into one helium-4 nucleus, with the small mass difference released as energy.

questions.text: The solar neutrino problem was resolved by the discovery that neutrinos:
questions.answer: oscillate between types and have mass
options: oscillate between types and have mass

questions.text: Fusion joins light nuclei, while fission splits heavy nuclei.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a terrestrial planet?
questions.answer: Jupiter

questions.text: Most asteroids orbit the Sun in a belt located between:
questions.answer: Mars and Jupiter

questions.text: Giant planets are generally larger but less dense than terrestrial planets.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Pluto is classified as a terrestrial planet.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: A small icy body that grows a glowing head and tail near the Sun is a ______.
questions.answer: comet

questions.text: The rotating cloud of gas and dust from which the solar system formed is the solar ______.
questions.answer: nebula

questions.text: Explain how a planet's density gives clues about what it is made of.
questions.answer: Density is mass divided by volume. Rock and metal are dense, so a high-density planet (like the terrestrial planets) must be mostly rock and metal. Gases and ices are far less dense, so a low-density planet (like the giant planets) must be made largely of those lighter materials.

questions.text: Why is Pluto considered a dwarf planet rather than a full planet?
questions.answer: Under the IAU definition, a full planet must orbit the Sun, be round due to its own gravity, AND have cleared its orbital neighborhood of other bodies. Pluto satisfies the first two but shares its region with many other Kuiper Belt objects, so it fails the third criterion and is classified as a dwarf planet.

questions.text: Match each region or body to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Rocky bodies between Mars and Jupiter
Disk of icy bodies beyond Neptune
Distant spherical source of long-period comets

questions.text: The giant planets are best described as:
questions.answer: Large, low-density, and gas-rich

questions.text: The solar system formed about 4.6 billion years ago.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Earth's magnetic field is generated by:
questions.answer: Motion of molten iron in the core (the dynamo effect)
options: Motion of molten iron in the core (the dynamo effect)

questions.text: Which layer of Earth lies between the crust and the core?
questions.answer: The mantle

questions.text: Seismic waves from earthquakes help reveal the structure of Earth's interior.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Without any greenhouse effect, Earth's surface would be warmer than it is today.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The theory that Earth's outer shell is broken into moving plates is called plate ______.
questions.answer: tectonics

questions.text: Earth's atmosphere is composed mostly of nitrogen and ______.
questions.answer: oxygen

questions.text: Explain how the greenhouse effect warms a planet's surface.
questions.answer: Sunlight (mostly visible) passes through the atmosphere and warms the surface, which then radiates energy back as infrared. Greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide and water vapor absorb that outgoing infrared and re-emit some of it back downward, trapping heat and raising the surface temperature above what it would be otherwise.

questions.text: Describe two distinct ways Earth's surface is reshaped over geologic time.
questions.answer: Internally, plate tectonics moves crustal plates, building mountains and causing earthquakes and volcanoes. Externally, erosion by wind, water, and ice wears down and redistributes surface material. Together these processes erase old features, which is why Earth has relatively few impact craters compared with the Moon.

questions.text: Match each Earth feature to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Thick rocky layer that slowly convects
Region shielding us from the solar wind

questions.text: The lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere, where weather occurs, is the:
questions.answer: Troposphere

questions.text: Earth has fewer visible impact craters than the Moon because tectonics and erosion erase them.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The dark, smooth plains on the Moon are called:
questions.answer: Maria

questions.text: The leading explanation for how the Moon formed is the:
questions.answer: Giant impact hypothesis

questions.text: A heavily cratered surface is generally older than a smooth one.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The Moon and Mercury have thick atmospheres that protect them from impacts.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The loose layer of broken rock and dust on an airless world is called ______.
questions.answer: regolith

questions.text: Mercury has an unusually large ______ core for its size.
questions.answer: iron

questions.text: Explain how counting craters lets astronomers estimate the relative age of a planetary surface.
questions.answer: Impacts occur over time, so craters accumulate on an undisturbed surface. A region with many craters has been exposed for a long time (old), while a region with few craters has been resurfaced more recently by lava, erosion, or tectonics (young). Comparing crater densities lets astronomers rank surfaces by relative age.

questions.text: Summarize the giant impact hypothesis for the Moon's formation and one piece of evidence that supports it.
questions.answer: Early in solar system history a Mars-sized body collided with the young Earth; debris blasted into orbit coalesced to form the Moon. Supporting evidence includes the Moon's relatively small iron core and its composition being similar to Earth's mantle, consistent with the Moon forming mostly from Earth's outer layers after the metallic cores had already merged.

questions.text: Match each lunar/Mercurian feature to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Long cliff from crustal shrinking on Mercury
Loose surface layer of rock and dust

questions.text: The long cliffs called scarps on Mercury are evidence that the planet:
questions.answer: Cooled and contracted

questions.text: Permanently shadowed craters near the poles of the Moon and Mercury may hold water ice.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Venus's extremely high surface temperature is caused mainly by:
questions.answer: A runaway greenhouse effect

questions.text: Which feature is found on Mars?
questions.answer: Olympus Mons

questions.text: Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most planets (retrograde).
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Mars currently has a thick atmosphere similar to Earth's.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The largest volcano in the solar system, located on Mars, is Olympus ______.
questions.answer: Mons

questions.text: The atmospheres of both Venus and Mars are composed mostly of carbon ______.
questions.answer: dioxide

questions.text: Explain why Venus is hotter than Mercury even though Mercury is closer to the Sun.
questions.answer: Mercury is closer to the Sun but is essentially airless, so heat escapes readily and its surface cools dramatically on its night side. Venus has a dense carbon-dioxide atmosphere that traps infrared radiation in a runaway greenhouse effect, raising and holding its surface temperature near 460 deg C everywhere, hotter than Mercury's dayside.

questions.text: What lines of evidence indicate that Mars had liquid water in the past, and why does this matter?
questions.answer: Spacecraft have found dried river valleys and outflow channels, sediment layers from ancient lakes, and minerals that form only in the presence of water. This matters because liquid water is considered essential for life, so past water makes Mars a key target in the search for past or present life.

questions.text: Match each planet feature to the correct world.
questions.answer: Match

questions.text: Compared with Earth, the surface atmospheric pressure on Mars is:
questions.answer: Less than 1 percent

questions.text: Galileo's observation of the phases of Venus supported the heliocentric model.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Jupiter and Saturn are composed mostly of:
questions.answer: Hydrogen and helium

questions.text: Which planet rotates almost on its side, with an axial tilt near 90 degrees?
questions.answer: Uranus

questions.text: The Great Red Spot is a long-lived storm in Jupiter's atmosphere larger than Earth.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Saturn's average density is greater than that of water.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: Hydrogen that conducts electricity like a metal under extreme pressure is called ______ hydrogen.
questions.answer: metallic

questions.text: Uranus and Neptune are classified as ______ giants because they contain more water, ammonia, and methane.
questions.answer: ice

questions.text: Explain why Jupiter and Saturn have such strong magnetic fields.
questions.answer: Deep inside Jupiter and Saturn, hydrogen is compressed so much that it becomes metallic hydrogen, an electrical conductor. As these large planets rotate rapidly and this conducting fluid circulates, a powerful dynamo is generated, producing very strong magnetic fields.

questions.text: What does it mean that Jupiter, Saturn, and Neptune radiate more energy than they receive, and what does it imply?
questions.answer: They emit more heat than they absorb from sunlight, which means they have internal heat sources. This leftover heat comes from their formation and ongoing slow contraction (and, for Saturn, helium settling), powering their winds, storms, and convective banded atmospheres.

questions.text: Match each giant planet to a distinguishing feature.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Largest planet; the Great Red Spot
Spectacular rings; density less than water
Most distant; deep blue, strong winds

questions.text: The alternating dark belts and light zones of Jupiter are produced by:
questions.answer: Rising and sinking gas in a convective atmosphere
options: Rising and sinking gas in a convective atmosphere

questions.text: The giant planets are noticeably flattened (oblate) because they rotate rapidly.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Planetary rings are composed mainly of:
questions.answer: Countless small particles of ice and rock
options: Countless small particles of ice and rock

questions.text: The most volcanically active body in the solar system is:
questions.answer: Io

questions.text: Europa is thought to have a subsurface ocean of liquid water beneath its icy crust.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Titan has no atmosphere at all.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The distance within which a planet's tidal forces would tear apart a large moon is the ______ limit.
questions.answer: Roche

questions.text: Io's intense volcanism is powered by tidal ______ from Jupiter's gravity.
questions.answer: heating

questions.text: Explain how tidal heating can keep a small moon like Io geologically active.
questions.answer: As Io orbits Jupiter, the giant planet's gravity varies in strength and direction (helped by gravitational tugs from neighboring moons), repeatedly flexing Io's interior. This constant flexing generates friction and heat deep inside, which keeps Io molten enough to sustain continuous, intense volcanic activity despite its small size.

questions.text: Why are moons with subsurface oceans, such as Europa and Enceladus, important in the search for life?
questions.answer: Liquid water is considered essential for life as we know it. Although these moons are far from the Sun and icy on the surface, tidal heating can maintain oceans of liquid water beneath their ice. Enceladus even vents water into space, letting spacecraft sample it. These hidden oceans make such moons promising places to look for life beyond Earth.

questions.text: Match each moon to its key feature.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Icy crust over a water ocean

questions.text: Pluto is best classified as a:
questions.answer: Dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt
options: Dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt

questions.text: A shepherd moon helps confine ring particles into narrow bands.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: What are the key steps of the scientific method, and how does each step contribute to the process of scientific inquiry?
questions.answer: The core steps are: observation (notice a phenomenon and raise a question), hypothesis (propose a testable explanation), prediction (state what should be true if the hypothesis holds), testing (gather evidence by observation or experiment), and analysis/revision (compare results to predictions and confirm, refine, or reject the hypothesis). Each step keeps the process tied to evidence; repeated successful testing can elevate a hypothesis into a theory.

questions.text: How do hypotheses differ from theories, and what role does each play in the scientific method?
questions.answer: A hypothesis is a tentative, testable explanation for a specific observation; it is the starting point that gets tested. A theory is a broad, well-tested framework supported by a large body of evidence that explains many observations and makes reliable predictions. Hypotheses feed the testing cycle; theories are the durable conclusions that survive it.

questions.text: How do astronomers use light-years to measure astronomical distances, and why is this unit important?
questions.answer: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year (~9.5 trillion km). Astronomers use it because interstellar distances are so vast that kilometers or AU become unwieldy. It keeps the numbers manageable and also conveys look-back time, since light from a star N light-years away left it N years ago.

questions.text: What is the significance of the astronomical unit (AU), and how is it used to describe distances within our solar system?
questions.answer: One AU is the average Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). It is a convenient yardstick for solar-system scales (e.g., Jupiter is about 5.2 AU from the Sun) and is the natural distance unit in Kepler's third law (P^2 = a^3 when P is in years and a in AU).

questions.text: Why is the concept of scale important when visualizing the vast distances between objects in space?
questions.answer: Cosmic distances and sizes span such enormous ranges that everyday intuition fails. Using scale models and analogies helps convey relative sizes and separations and corrects common misconceptions, such as how empty space is and how far apart stars actually are compared to their sizes.

questions.text: Give four ways to demonstrate that Earth is spherical.
questions.answer: (1) Ships disappear hull-first over the horizon as they sail away. (2) Earth casts a round shadow on the Moon during every lunar eclipse. (3) Different constellations are visible from different latitudes, and a star's altitude changes as you travel north or south. (4) Circumnavigation and modern photographs from space directly show a sphere.

questions.text: Explain, according to both geocentric and heliocentric cosmologies, why we see retrograde motion of the planets.
questions.answer: In Ptolemy's geocentric model, retrograde loops were produced by epicycles: each planet moved on a small circle whose center moved along a larger circle around Earth. In the Copernican heliocentric model, retrograde motion is an illusion caused by Earth (on a faster inner orbit) overtaking a slower outer planet, which makes that planet appear to drift backward against the stars for a time.

questions.text: In what ways did the work of Copernicus and Galileo differ from the views of the ancient Greeks and of their contemporaries?
questions.answer: Copernicus placed the Sun, not Earth, at the center, breaking from the Earth-centered cosmos of Aristotle and Ptolemy. Galileo used the new telescope to gather fresh empirical evidence (Jupiter's moons, the phases of Venus, lunar craters) and argued from observation rather than from philosophical authority or tradition.

questions.text: What were four of Galileo's discoveries that were important to astronomy?
questions.answer: (1) Four moons orbiting Jupiter, showing not everything circles Earth. (2) The full set of phases of Venus, which supports a Sun-centered system. (3) Craters and mountains on the Moon, showing the heavens are not perfect. (4) That the Milky Way is made of countless stars (and his observation of sunspots).

questions.text: Explain the origin of the magnitude designation for stellar brightness. Why does it seem to go backward, with smaller numbers indicating brighter stars?
questions.answer: Hipparchus ranked naked-eye stars from 1st magnitude (brightest) to 6th magnitude (faintest), so brighter meant a smaller number. The scale was later made precise and logarithmic but kept that inherited convention, so smaller and even negative magnitudes still denote brighter objects.

questions.text: Ursa Minor's stars are circumpolar from most of the Northern Hemisphere. Does that mean these stars are also above the horizon during the day?
questions.answer: Yes. Circumpolar stars never set, so they are above the horizon both day and night. We simply cannot see them in the daytime because the Sun's scattered light makes the sky too bright.

questions.text: Explain how the zodiacal constellations are different from the other constellations.
questions.answer: The zodiacal constellations lie along the ecliptic, the apparent yearly path of the Sun. Because the Sun, Moon, and planets all move close to the ecliptic, they appear to pass through these constellations. Other constellations lie away from the ecliptic and are never crossed by the Sun, Moon, or planets.

questions.text: The Sun was once thought to be a planet. Explain why.
questions.answer: To ancient observers, the Sun moved against the background of the fixed stars along the ecliptic, just as the planets (the 'wanderers') did. Since 'planet' meant wandering body, the Sun was naturally grouped with them.

questions.text: Is the ecliptic the same thing as the celestial equator? Explain.
questions.answer: No. The celestial equator is the projection of Earth's equator onto the sky, while the ecliptic is the Sun's apparent yearly path. They are tilted about 23.5 degrees to each other because of Earth's axial tilt, and they cross at the two equinox points.

questions.text: What is an asterism? Can you name an example?
questions.answer: An asterism is a recognizable pattern of stars that is not one of the 88 official constellations. The Big Dipper (part of the constellation Ursa Major) is a classic example; the Summer Triangle is another.

questions.text: Why did Pythagoras believe that Earth should be spherical?
questions.answer: His reasoning was largely philosophical and aesthetic: the sphere was regarded as the most perfect and harmonious shape, so a well-ordered Earth and cosmos ought to be spherical. Some observational hints reinforced the idea.

questions.text: How did Aristotle deduce that the Sun is farther away from Earth than the Moon?
questions.answer: He noted that the Moon passes in front of (occults) the Sun during solar eclipses and also moves in front of stars and planets, showing the Moon is the nearer body. Reasoning from eclipse geometry and the Moon's motion placed the Moon closer than the Sun.

questions.text: How did Hipparchus discover the wobble of Earth's axis, known as precession?
questions.answer: By comparing his own careful star-position measurements with much older records, he found that the stars' coordinates had shifted systematically over the centuries. This gradual drift revealed the slow conical wobble of Earth's rotation axis.

questions.text: Why did Ptolemy have to introduce multiple circles of motion for the planets instead of a single simple circle?
questions.answer: A single circle centered on Earth could not reproduce the planets' varying speeds, changing brightness, and retrograde loops. Ptolemy added epicycles, deferents, and equants so the geocentric model could match the complex observed motions.

questions.text: Why did Copernicus want to develop a completely new system for predicting planetary positions? Provide two reasons.
questions.answer: (1) The Ptolemaic system had become complicated and inelegant, relying on many epicycles and an equant he found unsatisfying. (2) A Sun-centered arrangement more naturally explained retrograde motion and gave a sensible ordering and spacing of the planets.

questions.text: What two factors made it difficult, at first, to choose between the Copernican heliocentric model and the Ptolemaic geocentric model?
