A stable balance (hydrostatic equilibrium) exists whenever the star is fusing fuel in its core, such as during the main sequence and the core helium-burning phases; in remnants, degeneracy pressure provides the support instead.

questions.text: Why are star clusters so useful for studying stellar evolution?
questions.answer: The stars in a cluster all formed at about the same time, from the same material, at the same distance, so they share an age and composition and differ mainly in mass. Comparing them isolates the effect of mass on evolution and lets astronomers determine the cluster's age.

questions.text: Would the Sun more likely have been a member of a globular cluster or an open cluster in the past?
questions.answer: An open cluster. The Sun is a relatively young, metal-rich Population I star, whereas globular clusters contain very old, metal-poor Population II stars formed early in the Galaxy's history.

questions.text: Two clusters: diagram A has most stars on the upper-left main sequence (rest off it); diagram B has most on the lower-right main sequence (rest off it). Which is older, and why?
questions.answer: Diagram B is the older cluster. In an old cluster the massive, hot, upper-left main-sequence stars have already evolved off the main sequence and died, leaving mainly low-mass stars on the lower-right of the main sequence; diagram A still has its short-lived massive stars, so it is younger.

questions.text: Why is the helium-to-carbon fusion process called the 'triple-alpha process,' and why must it occur at much higher temperature than hydrogen fusion?
questions.answer: It is called triple-alpha because three helium-4 nuclei (alpha particles) combine to form a carbon nucleus. It requires much higher temperature than hydrogen fusion because helium nuclei each carry a charge of +2, so their mutual electrical repulsion is stronger and more thermal energy is needed for them to collide and fuse.

questions.text: Describe the two 'recycling' mechanisms associated with stars.
questions.answer: During and at the end of their lives, stars return processed material enriched in heavy elements to the interstellar medium through stellar winds, planetary nebulae, and supernova explosions. That enriched gas is then incorporated into new generations of stars and planets, recycling material from one stellar generation to the next.

questions.text: In which group would you most likely find the least heavy-element abundance: open clusters, globular clusters, or associations?
questions.answer: Globular clusters. They are very old, having formed early in the Galaxy's history before much heavy-element enrichment occurred, so their stars (Population II) are the most metal-poor.

questions.text: Explain how an H-R diagram of a cluster's stars can be used to determine the cluster's age.
questions.answer: Astronomers find the main-sequence turnoff, the point where the most massive stars still on the main sequence are about to evolve off. Since more massive stars burn out faster, the location of the turnoff indicates how much time has passed: a lower, cooler turnoff means more massive stars have already died, so the cluster is older.

questions.text: Where did the carbon in a tree and the neon in 'neon lights' originally come from?
questions.answer: Both were forged inside stars by nuclear fusion, carbon mainly in red giants through the triple-alpha process and neon in massive stars, and then scattered into space by stellar winds and supernovae. That enriched material was later incorporated into the Sun, the Earth, and ultimately living things and everyday matter.

questions.text: How does a white dwarf differ from a neutron star? How does each form, and what keeps each from collapsing?
questions.answer: A white dwarf is the Earth-sized carbon-oxygen remnant left when a low- or intermediate-mass star sheds its outer layers; it is held up by electron degeneracy pressure. A neutron star is the roughly 20-km collapsed core left after a massive star's supernova, made almost entirely of neutrons and held up by neutron degeneracy pressure. They form from different-mass progenitors, and in each case quantum degeneracy pressure halts further collapse.

questions.text: Describe the evolution of a Sun-like star from the main sequence until it becomes a white dwarf.
questions.answer: It fuses hydrogen on the main sequence, then becomes a red giant with shell hydrogen burning, ignites helium in its core, and later swells into an asymptotic giant with helium- and hydrogen-burning shells. Finally it ejects its outer layers as a planetary nebula, leaving behind the hot, exposed carbon-oxygen core as a white dwarf that slowly cools.

questions.text: Describe the evolution of a 20-solar-mass star up to its supernova, and how it differs from the Sun.
questions.answer: A massive star fuses progressively heavier elements in concentric shells (hydrogen, helium, carbon, oxygen, neon, silicon) until it builds an iron core. Iron fusion absorbs rather than releases energy, so the core collapses catastrophically into a neutron star or black hole, and the rebound and neutrino burst blow the star apart as a Type II supernova. Unlike the Sun, its high mass and temperature let it fuse far beyond carbon and end in a violent explosion.

questions.text: How do the two types of supernovae differ, and what kind of star gives rise to each?
questions.answer: A Type Ia supernova comes from a white dwarf in a binary that accretes matter, exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit, and detonates completely; its spectrum shows no hydrogen. A Type II supernova comes from the core collapse of a single massive star and shows hydrogen lines in its spectrum.

questions.text: A star starts at 5 solar masses but ends as a 0.8-solar-mass white dwarf. During which stages did it lose mass, and how?
questions.answer: It lost most of its mass through strong stellar winds during the red-giant and especially the asymptotic-giant-branch phases, and then by ejecting its outer envelope as a planetary nebula at the very end. These processes carried away the difference between its starting mass and the final white-dwarf mass.

questions.text: If a neutron star's formation causes a supernova, why are only a few of the hundreds of known pulsars found in supernova remnants?
questions.answer: Supernova remnants disperse and fade within a few tens of thousands of years, while pulsars keep emitting for millions of years, so most pulsars long outlive their visible remnants. In addition, the explosion often gives the neutron star a 'kick' that flings it out of the remnant.

questions.text: How can the Crab Nebula shine with the power of about 100,000 Suns when its star exploded nearly 1000 years ago? Who 'pays the bills'?
questions.answer: The central pulsar, a rapidly spinning neutron star, supplies the energy. As it gradually slows, its enormous rotational energy is channeled through its strong magnetic field into the surrounding gas, continuously powering the nebula's glow long after the explosion.

questions.text: How is a nova different from a Type Ia supernova, and from a Type II supernova?
questions.answer: A nova is a white dwarf in a binary that briefly ignites a layer of accreted hydrogen on its surface; the dwarf survives and can erupt repeatedly. A Type Ia supernova is the complete thermonuclear destruction of a white dwarf pushed past the Chandrasekhar limit, and a Type II is the core collapse of a massive star. A nova is vastly less energetic and, unlike the supernovae, is not destructive.

questions.text: Apart from mass, how are binary systems with a neutron star different from those with a white dwarf?
questions.answer: A neutron star's gravity is far stronger because it is so much smaller and denser, so matter falling onto it releases much more energy, often as intense X-rays and X-ray bursts, and can power pulsars. White-dwarf binaries instead produce novae and Type Ia supernovae, with far less energetic accretion.

questions.text: What observations from SN 1987A helped confirm theories about supernovae?
questions.answer: Detectors on Earth recorded a burst of neutrinos arriving just before the visible light, confirming that a core collapse releases most of its energy as neutrinos, exactly as theory predicted. SN 1987A also let astronomers watch a supernova evolve in detail and identify its progenitor, which turned out to be a blue supergiant.

questions.text: Describe how a white dwarf evolves over time, particularly its luminosity, temperature, and radius.
questions.answer: A white dwarf no longer generates energy by fusion, so it simply radiates away its stored heat and cools over billions of years. Its luminosity and temperature steadily decrease while its radius stays nearly constant, and in principle it eventually becomes a cold, dark black dwarf.

questions.text: Describe how a pulsar evolves over time, particularly its rotation and pulse signal.
questions.answer: As a pulsar ages it gradually loses rotational energy, so it spins more slowly and its pulses arrive less frequently and grow weaker. Eventually it slows so much that it no longer emits detectable pulses and effectively switches off.

questions.text: How would a white dwarf from a 1-solar-mass star differ from one formed from a 6-solar-mass star?
questions.answer: Both end as white dwarfs below the Chandrasekhar limit, but the 6-solar-mass star evolves far faster and loses much more mass, and it may leave a somewhat more massive white dwarf with a different composition (such as oxygen and neon rather than carbon and oxygen). The 1-solar-mass star evolves slowly and leaves a typical carbon-oxygen white dwarf.

questions.text: What do astronomers think causes longer-duration and shorter-duration gamma-ray bursts?
questions.answer: Long-duration gamma-ray bursts are thought to come from the collapse of very massive, rapidly rotating stars into black holes (collapsars or hypernovae). Short-duration bursts are thought to come from the merger of two compact objects, such as two neutron stars or a neutron star and a black hole.

questions.text: How did astronomers finally solve the mystery of what gamma-ray bursts were, and what instruments were required?
questions.answer: The breakthrough came when satellites could quickly pinpoint a burst's position accurately enough for follow-up telescopes to catch its fading afterglow. X-ray satellites such as BeppoSAX (and later Swift), combined with rapid optical observations, located the bursts in distant galaxies, proving they are enormously energetic cosmological events.

questions.text: How does the equivalence principle lead us to suspect that spacetime might be curved?
questions.answer: The equivalence principle says that gravity and acceleration are locally indistinguishable. Since a light beam appears to bend in an accelerating frame, it must also bend in a gravitational field, which implies that mass curves spacetime and that light follows that curvature.

questions.text: If general relativity best describes gravity, why do physicists still use Newton's equations (for example, in building a bridge)?
questions.answer: Newton's equations are far simpler and are accurate to extremely high precision for weak gravity and ordinary speeds, which covers everyday engineering and most spaceflight. General relativity's corrections are utterly negligible in those situations, so there is no need for its added complexity.

questions.text: Describe three experiments that verified predictions of general relativity after Einstein proposed it.
questions.answer: First, the bending of starlight passing near the Sun, measured during the 1919 solar eclipse. Second, the extra precession of Mercury's orbit (its perihelion advance), which Newtonian gravity could not fully explain. Third, the gravitational redshift and time dilation of light in a gravitational field, later confirmed by precise clocks and required for GPS to work.

questions.text: If a black hole itself emits no radiation, what evidence do astronomers have that black holes are real?
questions.answer: They detect black holes through their gravitational effects on surroundings: gas spiraling into a black hole heats up and emits X-rays, stars are seen orbiting unseen very massive companions, and gravitational waves from merging black holes have been recorded. The amount of mass inferred in such a tiny volume can only be a black hole, and the event-horizon image provides direct evidence.

questions.text: What characteristics must a binary star have to be a good black-hole candidate, and why does each matter?
questions.answer: One visible star should orbit an unseen companion whose mass, derived from the orbital motion, exceeds the neutron-star limit of about three solar masses, and the system should emit strong X-rays. The high mass rules out a white dwarf or neutron star, and the X-rays indicate matter falling into an extremely deep gravitational well, as expected for a black hole.

questions.text: A student jumps into a 10-solar-mass black hole. What is the trip like for him, and for the class watching from afar?
questions.answer: For the student, tidal forces stretch him head-to-toe ('spaghettification'); he crosses the event horizon (feeling nothing special at the crossing itself) and reaches the central singularity quickly by his own clock. For the class, his infalling image appears to slow down and freeze at the horizon while reddening and dimming, so they never actually see him cross, because of gravitational time dilation.

questions.text: What is an event horizon? Does our Sun have one?
questions.answer: An event horizon is the boundary around a black hole within which nothing, not even light, can escape. The Sun does not have one, because it is nowhere near compressed within its Schwarzschild radius (only about 3 km); it would need to be crushed to that size to develop an event horizon.

questions.text: What is a gravitational wave, and why was it so hard to detect?
questions.answer: A gravitational wave is a ripple in the fabric of spacetime produced by accelerating masses, such as orbiting and merging compact objects, traveling outward at the speed of light. It was extremely hard to detect because the resulting distortions are minuscule, far smaller than an atomic nucleus over kilometers, requiring exquisitely sensitive laser interferometers like LIGO.

questions.text: What are some strong sources of gravitational waves that astronomers hope to detect?
questions.answer: The strongest sources are merging compact objects: pairs of black holes, pairs of neutron stars, and neutron-star-black-hole mergers. Other potential sources include supernova core collapses and processes in the very early universe.

questions.text: Suppose the mass of a black hole doubles. Does the event horizon change, and if so how?
questions.answer: Yes. The radius of the event horizon (the Schwarzschild radius) is directly proportional to the mass, so doubling the mass doubles the radius of the event horizon.

questions.text: Explain why we see the Milky Way as a faint band of light stretching across the sky.
questions.answer: We live inside the flat disk of our own galaxy, so when we look along the plane of that disk our line of sight passes through enormous numbers of distant stars. Their combined light blends into the hazy band we call the Milky Way; looking out of the disk, away from the band, we see far fewer stars.

questions.text: Explain where in a spiral galaxy you would expect to find globular clusters, molecular clouds, and atomic hydrogen.
questions.answer: Globular clusters are found in the spherical halo surrounding the galaxy, well above and below the disk. Molecular clouds and atomic hydrogen gas lie in the thin disk, concentrated along the spiral arms where star formation occurs.

questions.text: Describe several characteristics that distinguish Population I from Population II stars.
questions.answer: Population I stars are relatively young and rich in heavy elements (like the Sun), found in the disk and spiral arms on fairly circular orbits. Population II stars are old and metal-poor, found in the halo and bulge, and move on more random, elongated orbits.

questions.text: Briefly describe the main parts of our Galaxy.
questions.answer: The Galaxy has a flat disk containing spiral arms, gas, dust, and young stars; a central bulge of older stars; and a surrounding spherical halo containing globular clusters and old stars. It is embedded in a much larger halo of dark matter, with a supermassive black hole at its nucleus.

questions.text: Describe the evidence indicating that a black hole may be at the center of our Galaxy.
questions.answer: Astronomers have tracked individual stars near the galactic center orbiting an invisible, compact object at very high speeds. Their orbits reveal about four million solar masses concentrated in a tiny region, which can only be a supermassive black hole, identified with the radio source Sagittarius A*.

questions.text: Explain why the abundances of heavy elements in stars correlate with their positions in the Galaxy.
questions.answer: Each generation of stars enriches the surrounding gas with heavy elements when it dies. The disk has had ongoing star formation that steadily raised its metal content, while the halo formed early, before much enrichment, so its stars are metal-poor; thus heavy-element abundance correlates with location and the age of the stars there.

questions.text: What will be the long-term future of our Galaxy?
questions.answer: The Milky Way will keep forming stars, though more slowly as its gas is consumed. It is also approaching the Andromeda galaxy, and in a few billion years the two will collide and merge into a single larger galaxy, likely a giant elliptical.

questions.text: Describe the main distinguishing features of spiral, elliptical, and irregular galaxies.
questions.answer: Spiral galaxies have a flattened disk with spiral arms, a central bulge, abundant gas and dust, and active star formation. Elliptical galaxies are smooth, rounded systems of mostly old stars with little gas or ongoing star formation. Irregular galaxies lack any regular shape and are often gas-rich with vigorous star formation.

questions.text: Why did it take so long for the existence of other galaxies to be established?
questions.answer: Other galaxies are extraordinarily far away and appear only as faint, fuzzy patches that were long thought to be nebulae within the Milky Way. It was not until the 1920s, when Hubble measured Cepheid variables in Andromeda, that their great distances proved they lie far outside our Galaxy, settling the 'Great Debate.'

questions.text: Explain what the mass-to-light ratio is and why it is smaller in star-forming spirals than in ellipticals.
questions.answer: The mass-to-light ratio is a galaxy's total mass divided by its light output. Star-forming spirals contain bright, massive young stars that emit a great deal of light per unit mass, giving a low ratio, whereas ellipticals contain only old, faint stars that emit little light per unit mass, giving a higher ratio.

questions.text: If dwarf ellipticals are the most common type of galaxy, why did they escape our notice for so long?
questions.answer: Dwarf ellipticals are small and extremely faint, with very low surface brightness. Despite being the most numerous galaxies, their dimness makes them hard to detect, especially at large distances, so they were long overlooked.

questions.text: What are the two best ways to measure the distance to a nearby spiral galaxy?
questions.answer: For a nearby spiral, the best methods are Cepheid variable stars, using the period-luminosity relation to get their true brightness, and the Tully-Fisher relation, which links a spiral's rotation speed to its luminosity. Both give the luminosity, which compared to apparent brightness yields the distance.

questions.text: What are the two best ways to measure the distance to a distant, isolated spiral galaxy?
questions.answer: For a distant spiral, Type Ia supernovae work well because they are very luminous standard candles visible across great distances, as does the Tully-Fisher relation. Cepheids cannot be used because individual stars are too faint to resolve that far away.

questions.text: Why is Hubble's law considered one of the most important discoveries in the history of astronomy?
questions.answer: Hubble's law shows that galaxies recede at speeds proportional to their distance (v = H0 times d), which is the key evidence that the universe is expanding. This underpins Big Bang cosmology, provides a way to estimate distances to remote galaxies, and lets astronomers estimate the age of the universe.

questions.text: What does it mean to say the universe is expanding? Is your classroom expanding? The solar system?
questions.answer: It means space itself is stretching, so the distances between distant galaxies and clusters grow over time. Your classroom and the solar system are not expanding, because they are held together by their own gravity and electromagnetic forces, which easily overcome the expansion; only on the largest scales, between systems not bound together, does the expansion dominate.

questions.text: Was Hubble's original estimate of the distance to the Andromeda galaxy correct?
questions.answer: No. His original distance was too small, roughly half the modern value, because the Cepheid calibration he used was in error (there are two types of Cepheids with different luminosities). Andromeda is actually much farther away than Hubble first calculated.

questions.text: Does an elliptical galaxy rotate like a spiral galaxy?
questions.answer: No. Unlike a spiral, whose stars and gas orbit in an organized rotating disk, an elliptical galaxy's stars move on randomly oriented, three-dimensional orbits. The galaxy is supported by these random stellar motions rather than by ordered rotation.

questions.text: Why does the disk of a spiral galaxy appear dark when viewed edge on?
questions.answer: The disk contains lanes of dust that absorb and block light. When we view a spiral edge-on, our line of sight passes through this dust, which obscures the stars behind it and produces a dark band across the middle of the galaxy.

questions.text: What causes the largest mass-to-light ratio: gas and dust, dark matter, or burnt-out stars?
questions.answer: Dark matter. It contributes a great deal of mass but emits no light at all, so systems whose mass is dominated by dark matter have the highest mass-to-light ratios.

questions.text: Two isolated spiral galaxies have the same apparent brightness but rotate at different rates. What can you say about their relative luminosity and distance?
questions.answer: By the Tully-Fisher relation, the faster-rotating galaxy is intrinsically more luminous. If the two appear equally bright in the sky, the more luminous (faster-rotating) one must be the more distant of the two.

questions.text: If all distant galaxies are expanding away from us, does this mean we are at the center of the universe?
questions.answer: No. In a uniformly expanding universe, every observer in every galaxy sees all the other galaxies receding from them, just as every dot on an inflating balloon sees the others move away. There is no special center, and our vantage point is not unique.

questions.text: Is the Hubble constant actually constant?
questions.answer: It is constant in the sense of having the same value everywhere in space at a given time, which is why it appears in Hubble's law. However, its value changes over cosmic time as the expansion rate of the universe evolves, so it is not constant throughout the history of the universe.

questions.text: What are the basic observations about the universe that any theory of cosmology must explain?
questions.answer: A successful cosmology must explain that the universe is expanding (Hubble's law), the existence of the cosmic microwave background radiation, the observed abundances of the light elements such as hydrogen and helium, and the large-scale uniformity of the universe together with its structure of galaxies and clusters.

questions.text: Describe some possible futures for the universe, and what property determines which is correct.
questions.answer: The universe could expand forever, slow and eventually recollapse in a 'Big Crunch,' or expand at an accelerating rate. Which outcome occurs depends on the universe's total density and energy content, including dark energy; current observations show the expansion is accelerating, indicating it will expand forever.

questions.text: What does the term 'Hubble time' mean, and what is the current best calculation for it?
questions.answer: The Hubble time is the inverse of the Hubble constant (1 divided by H0); it estimates the age of the universe assuming the expansion rate had always been constant. The current best value is roughly 14 billion years, close to the true age of about 13.8 billion years.

questions.text: Which formed first: hydrogen nuclei or hydrogen atoms?
questions.answer: Hydrogen nuclei (protons) formed first, within the first moments of the universe. Complete hydrogen atoms, with electrons bound to the nuclei, did not form until about 380,000 years later, when the universe had cooled enough for electrons and nuclei to combine (the era of recombination).

questions.text: Describe at least two characteristics of the universe that are explained by the Big Bang model.
questions.answer: The Big Bang model explains the observed expansion of the universe described by Hubble's law, and it predicts the cosmic microwave background radiation as the cooled afterglow of the hot early universe. It also accounts for the observed primordial abundances of light elements, such as the roughly one-quarter helium by mass.

questions.text: Describe two properties of the universe not explained by the standard Big Bang (without inflation), and how inflation explains them.
questions.answer: The standard model does not explain the flatness problem (why the universe's geometry is so nearly flat) or the horizon problem (why widely separated regions have nearly identical temperatures). Inflation, a brief burst of extremely rapid expansion in the early universe, solves both by stretching any curvature flat and by placing now-distant regions in causal contact before they were driven apart.

questions.text: Why do astronomers believe there must be dark matter? What is dark energy?
questions.answer: Dark matter is inferred because galaxies rotate far faster than their visible mass can explain and galaxy clusters need much more gravity than their luminous matter provides to stay bound; this unseen mass is dark matter. Dark energy is the mysterious form of energy that is driving the accelerating expansion of the universe.

questions.text: Using Einstein's ideas of space and time, explain why all galaxies outside our Local Group show a redshift.
questions.answer: In general relativity space itself expands, and as light travels through expanding space its wavelength is stretched, shifting it toward the red (cosmological redshift). This is not motion through space but the stretching of space itself, so light from all distant galaxies arrives redshifted; nearby galaxies in our Local Group are gravitationally bound to us and do not partake in this expansion.

questions.text: There is more helium in the universe than stars could have made in 13.8 billion years. How does the Big Bang solve this?
questions.answer: The Big Bang model includes a few minutes of intense nuclear reactions in the hot early universe (Big Bang nucleosynthesis) that converted about a quarter of the matter into helium before any stars existed. This primordial helium accounts for the large amount that stellar fusion alone could not have produced.

questions.text: Describe the anthropic principle. What are some properties of the universe that make it 'ready' for life?
questions.answer: The anthropic principle notes that the universe's properties must be compatible with the existence of observers like us, since otherwise we would not be here to observe it. Examples of fine-tuned conditions include the strengths of the fundamental forces, the specific nuclear resonance that lets stars produce carbon, and an expansion rate that allowed galaxies and stars to form.

questions.text: Describe the evidence that the expansion of the universe is accelerating.
questions.answer: Observations of distant Type Ia supernovae found them to be fainter, and thus farther away, than expected if the expansion were slowing down. This indicates the expansion has actually been speeding up over time, an effect attributed to dark energy.

questions.text: A star's apparent brightness depends on its luminosity and its:
questions.answer: distance

questions.text: Which spectral class is the hottest?
questions.answer: O

questions.text: In the magnitude system, brighter stars have smaller numbers.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The main reason stellar spectra differ is differences in composition, not temperature.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: A star's true total energy output per second is its ______.
questions.answer: luminosity

questions.text: The classification of stars by temperature uses the sequence O, B, A, F, G, K, and ______.
questions.answer: M

questions.text: Explain how astronomers determine a star's temperature and chemical makeup from its spectrum.
questions.answer: A star's color and the pattern of its spectral lines reveal its temperature, because temperature controls which atoms are ionized or excited and therefore which lines appear and how strong they are. The specific set of lines present identifies the chemical elements in the star, since each element has a unique spectral fingerprint.

questions.text: Why can two stars with the same apparent brightness have very different luminosities?
questions.answer: Apparent brightness combines luminosity and distance, falling off as one over distance squared. A very luminous star that is far away can look just as bright as a modest star that is nearby, so equal apparent brightness does not imply equal luminosity unless the distances are also equal.

questions.text: A redshift in a star's spectral lines indicates the star is:
questions.answer: moving away from us

questions.text: On the H-R diagram, the diagonal band where most stars (including the Sun) are found is the:
questions.answer: main sequence

questions.text: The masses of stars are most directly measured using:
questions.answer: binary star systems

questions.text: White dwarfs are hot but faint, placing them at the lower left of the H-R diagram.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Most stars in the Galaxy are more massive and luminous than the Sun.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: A diagram plotting luminosity against temperature for many stars is the ______ diagram.
questions.answer: H-R

questions.text: The most common type of star in the Galaxy is the red ______.
questions.answer: dwarf

questions.text: Explain why eclipsing binaries are especially valuable for determining stellar properties.
questions.answer: Because we view their orbit nearly edge-on, eclipsing binaries let astronomers combine the timing of the eclipses with the stars' orbital speeds to find each star's mass and diameter. Their spectra add temperatures, and their light curves yield luminosities, so a single eclipsing binary can reveal an unusually complete set of stellar properties.

questions.text: Describe how luminosity, temperature, mass, and radius vary along the main sequence.
questions.answer: Along the main sequence these properties change together. Toward the upper-left end, stars are hot, large, massive, and highly luminous; toward the lower-right end, they are cool, small, low in mass, and faint. The Sun sits near the middle of this sequence.

questions.text: Match each binary type to how it is detected.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Two stars seen separately through a telescope
Periodic Doppler shifts in the spectrum
Regular dips in brightness as stars eclipse
More massive main-sequence stars are more luminous

questions.text: A cool, low-mass, faint, and very common main-sequence star is called a:
questions.answer: red dwarf

questions.text: The distance method that uses Earth's orbital motion to measure a nearby star's apparent shift is:
questions.answer: parallax

questions.text: A pulsating star whose period reveals its luminosity is a:
questions.answer: Cepheid variable

questions.text: One parsec is about 3.26 light-years.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Parallax can be used to measure the distance to any star, no matter how far.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: An object of known luminosity used to measure distance is called a standard ______.
questions.answer: candle

questions.text: The pulsation ______ of a Cepheid reveals its luminosity.
questions.answer: period

questions.text: Describe the steps you would take to find the distance to a newly discovered Cepheid variable.
questions.answer: First measure the period over which its brightness pulsates. Use the Cepheid period-luminosity relation to convert that period into the star's true luminosity. Finally, compare its luminosity to its observed apparent brightness, using the inverse-square law, to compute the distance.

questions.text: Explain what the cosmic distance ladder is and why multiple methods are needed.
questions.answer: The cosmic distance ladder is the sequence of distance-measuring techniques, each calibrated using the method below it, that astronomers use to reach progressively greater distances. Multiple methods are needed because no single technique works at all scales: parallax serves nearby stars, Cepheids and other standard candles reach nearby galaxies, and Type Ia supernovae extend to very distant ones.

questions.text: Match each distance method to the situation it suits best.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: A star within a few hundred light-years
A nearby galaxy with pulsating stars
A non-variable star with a clear spectrum

questions.text: Apparent brightness decreases with the square of the distance; this is the:
questions.answer: inverse-square law

questions.text: Stars are born in:
questions.answer: cold molecular clouds

questions.text: An exoplanet detected by the dip in its star's light as it passes in front is found by the:
questions.answer: transit method

questions.text: More massive protostars reach the main sequence faster than low-mass ones.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Star formation is best observed with visible light because dust is transparent to it.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: A young, contracting clump of gas not yet fusing hydrogen is a ______.
questions.answer: protostar

questions.text: A giant planet orbiting very close to its star is called a hot ______.
questions.answer: Jupiter

questions.text: Trace what happens as a star forms, from a dense cloud core to the main sequence.
questions.answer: A dense core within a molecular cloud collapses under gravity, heating as it contracts and forming a protostar surrounded by an accretion disk. The protostar keeps contracting and heating until its center reaches about 10 million K, igniting hydrogen fusion; the energy released halts the contraction and the star settles onto the main sequence.

questions.text: Why was no exoplanet around a Sun-like star found until 1995, and how do such planets differ from ours?
questions.answer: Detecting a planet requires measuring the tiny stellar wobble or slight dimming it causes, which demanded spectroscopic precision not achieved until 1995 (51 Pegasi b). Many discovered systems differ from ours by hosting hot Jupiters, super-Earths, and eccentric or tightly packed orbits, prompting theories of planetary migration.

questions.text: The technique that detects an exoplanet by the Doppler wobble of its star is the:
questions.answer: radial-velocity method

questions.text: When a Sun-like star exhausts its core hydrogen, it first becomes a:
questions.answer: red giant

questions.text: The fusion of three helium nuclei into carbon is the:
questions.answer: triple-alpha process

questions.text: Most stars are observed on the main sequence because that phase lasts the longest.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: A cluster with a lower main-sequence turnoff is younger than one with a higher turnoff.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The point where stars leave the main sequence on a cluster's H-R diagram is the main-sequence ______.
questions.answer: turnoff

questions.text: Old, metal-poor, spherical clusters in the halo are ______ clusters.
questions.answer: globular

questions.text: Explain how an H-R diagram of a star cluster reveals its age.
questions.answer: Astronomers locate the main-sequence turnoff, the most massive stars still fusing hydrogen. Because more massive stars exhaust their fuel faster, the position of the turnoff dates the cluster: as time passes, ever-lower-mass stars reach the turnoff, so a turnoff at lower luminosity means an older cluster.

questions.text: Describe the two recycling processes that connect generations of stars.
questions.answer: During and at the end of their lives, stars return material enriched in heavy elements to the interstellar medium through winds, planetary nebulae, and supernovae. That enriched gas is then gathered into new molecular clouds and incorporated into later generations of stars and planets, recycling matter across stellar generations.

questions.text: Betelgeuse is more luminous than the Sun even though it is cooler because it is:
questions.answer: much larger

questions.text: A white dwarf is supported against gravity by:
questions.answer: electron degeneracy pressure

questions.text: A Type II supernova is produced by:
questions.answer: the core collapse of a massive star
options: the core collapse of a massive star

questions.text: The Chandrasekhar limit is about 1.4 solar masses.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: A nova completely destroys the white dwarf involved.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: A rapidly spinning, magnetized neutron star seen as regular pulses is a ______.
questions.answer: pulsar

questions.text: A Type ______ supernova comes from a white dwarf exceeding the Chandrasekhar limit.
questions.answer: Ia

questions.text: Explain how a massive star's interior leads to a core-collapse supernova.
questions.answer: A massive star fuses progressively heavier elements in concentric shells until it builds an inert iron core. Iron fusion absorbs rather than releases energy, so once the core exceeds its support limit it collapses catastrophically into a neutron star or black hole, and the resulting rebound and neutrino burst blow the star's outer layers apart as a Type II supernova.

questions.text: Contrast white dwarfs and neutron stars, including how each forms and what supports it.
questions.answer: A white dwarf is the Earth-sized carbon-oxygen remnant left when a low- or intermediate-mass star sheds its envelope, and it is supported by electron degeneracy pressure. A neutron star is the roughly 20-kilometer collapsed core left by a massive star's supernova, composed mostly of neutrons and supported by neutron degeneracy pressure. They come from different-mass progenitors and are held up by different forms of quantum pressure.

questions.text: Match each remnant or event to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Earth-sized remnant of a low-mass star
~20-km remnant of a massive star's core
White dwarf detonating past the Chandrasekhar limit
Spinning neutron star seen as regular pulses

questions.text: The energy that keeps the Crab Nebula glowing today comes from its central:
questions.answer: pulsar

questions.text: The boundary around a black hole from which nothing can escape is the:
questions.answer: event horizon

questions.text: General relativity describes gravity as:
questions.answer: the curvature of spacetime

questions.text: If a black hole's mass doubles, its event horizon radius also doubles.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The Sun currently has an event horizon around it.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The radius of a black hole's event horizon is called the ______ radius.
questions.answer: Schwarzschild

questions.text: Ripples in spacetime from accelerating masses are gravitational ______.
questions.answer: waves

questions.text: Since a black hole emits no radiation, explain how astronomers find evidence for one.
questions.answer: Astronomers detect black holes by their gravitational effects rather than directly. Gas falling toward a black hole forms a hot accretion disk that emits X-rays, stars can be seen orbiting an unseen companion whose mass exceeds the neutron-star limit, and mergers produce gravitational waves. The amount of mass inferred within such a tiny region can only be a black hole.

questions.text: Use the equivalence principle to explain why we expect spacetime to be curved.
questions.answer: The equivalence principle states that being in a gravitational field is locally indistinguishable from being in an accelerating reference frame. Because a light beam appears to bend in an accelerating frame, it must also bend in a gravitational field; the natural way to describe light bending under gravity is that mass curves spacetime and light follows that curvature.

questions.text: Astronomers infer a black hole in a binary when the unseen companion's mass exceeds about:
questions.answer: 3 solar masses

questions.text: We see the Milky Way as a band of light because:
questions.answer: we are inside its flat disk
options: it is a nearby star cloud
we are inside its flat disk

questions.text: Globular clusters in the Milky Way are found mainly in the:
questions.answer: halo

questions.text: Population II stars are old and metal-poor, found in the halo and bulge.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: The Milky Way will remain isolated and never interact with other galaxies.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The compact radio source marking the black hole at the Galaxy's center is Sagittarius ______.
questions.answer: A*

questions.text: Young, metal-rich disk stars like the Sun are called Population ______ stars.
questions.answer: I

questions.text: Describe the evidence that a supermassive black hole lies at the center of the Milky Way.
questions.answer: Astronomers have tracked individual stars near the galactic center as they orbit an invisible object at very high speeds. The shapes and speeds of those orbits reveal about four million solar masses packed into a region smaller than our solar system, which can only be a supermassive black hole, identified with the radio source Sagittarius A*.

questions.text: Explain why heavy-element abundance in stars correlates with their location in the Galaxy.
questions.answer: Each generation of stars enriches the surrounding gas with heavy elements as it dies. The disk has undergone continued star formation that steadily raised its metal content, while the halo formed early, before much enrichment, so its stars are metal-poor. As a result, metal-rich stars are found in the disk and metal-poor stars in the halo.

questions.text: Match each Galaxy component to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Flat, rotating, with spiral arms and young stars

questions.text: Molecular clouds and active star formation in the Milky Way are concentrated in the:
questions.answer: spiral arms of the disk

questions.text: A galaxy with a flattened disk, spiral arms, and active star formation is a(n):
questions.answer: spiral galaxy

questions.text: Hubble's law states that a galaxy's recession speed is proportional to its:
questions.answer: distance

questions.text: The expansion of the universe stretches space itself, so unbound galaxies grow farther apart.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Your classroom and the solar system are expanding along with the universe.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The relation v = H0 x d is known as ______'s law.
questions.answer: Hubble

questions.text: The most common type of galaxy is the dwarf ______.
questions.answer: elliptical

questions.text: Explain what it means to say the universe is expanding, and why the solar system is not expanding.
questions.answer: Cosmic expansion means that space itself is stretching, so the distances between galaxies and clusters not bound together grow over time. The solar system and smaller systems are not expanding because they are held together by gravity (and, on small scales, electromagnetic forces) that easily overcome the expansion; only on the largest scales, between unbound systems, does expansion dominate.

questions.text: How would you measure the distance to (a) a nearby spiral galaxy and (b) a very distant one?
questions.answer: For a nearby spiral, Cepheid variable stars (via the period-luminosity relation) and the Tully-Fisher relation work well. For a very distant spiral, Cepheids are too faint to resolve, so astronomers use Type Ia supernovae, which are extremely luminous standard candles visible across great distances, along with the Tully-Fisher relation.

questions.text: Match each item to its description.
questions.answer: Match
pairs: Disk with arms and star formation
Cause of the highest mass-to-light ratios

questions.text: The disk of a spiral galaxy looks dark when viewed edge-on because of:
questions.answer: dust in the disk plane

questions.text: The leftover radiation from the hot early universe is the:
questions.answer: cosmic microwave background

questions.text: Which formed first in the early universe?
questions.answer: hydrogen nuclei

questions.text: Inflation helps explain why the universe is so nearly flat and so uniform in temperature.
questions.answer: True

questions.text: Dark energy is the unseen mass that holds galaxies together.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: The leftover radiation from the early universe is the cosmic microwave ______.
questions.answer: background

questions.text: The energy driving the accelerating expansion of the universe is dark ______.
questions.answer: energy

questions.text: Explain how the Big Bang model accounts for the large amount of helium in the universe.
questions.answer: In the first few minutes after the Big Bang, the universe was hot and dense enough for nuclear reactions (Big Bang nucleosynthesis) to fuse protons and neutrons into helium, converting about a quarter of the matter into helium before any stars existed. This primordial helium explains the abundance that stellar fusion alone could not have produced in the age of the universe.

questions.text: What are the flatness and horizon problems, and how does inflation resolve them?
questions.answer: The flatness problem is the puzzle of why the universe's geometry is so close to flat, and the horizon problem is why regions too far apart to have ever exchanged light have the same temperature. Inflation, a brief burst of exponential expansion in the early universe, resolves both by stretching any initial curvature flat and by placing now-distant regions in causal contact before inflation drove them apart.

questions.text: Evidence that the expansion of the universe is accelerating comes from observations of distant:
questions.answer: Type Ia supernovae

questions.text: According to the video, science must above all be:
questions.answer: honest

questions.text: The video presents astrology as having real scientific predictive power.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: Explain the video's idea that 'astronomy puts you in your place.'
questions.answer: The lecture takes a tour outward from Earth through the solar system, the Milky Way, the Local Group, and on to the observable universe, showing how astonishingly small and ordinary our position is. Recognizing that vast scale is what the host means by astronomy 'putting you in your place.'
questions.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: Eratosthenes found the Sun was about 7 degrees off vertical at Alexandria, which is about what fraction of a full circle?
questions.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: Describe how Eratosthenes estimated the circumference of the Earth.
questions.answer: He knew the Sun was directly overhead a well at Syene at noon on the summer solstice, while at the same moment it was 7 degrees off vertical at Alexandria. Since 7 degrees is about one-fiftieth of a circle, the distance between the two cities (5,000 stadia) must be about one-fiftieth of Earth's circumference, giving roughly 250,000 stadia, or about 25,000 miles.
questions.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: Aristarchus was among the first to propose that Earth orbits the Sun.
questions.answer: True
questions.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: In the heliocentric model, the retrograde motion of Mars results from:
questions.answer: Earth overtaking Mars in orbit
questions.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: The tiny apparent shift of a nearby star due to Earth's orbital motion is called its ______.
questions.answer: parallax
questions.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: Why was the absence of an observed stellar parallax a reasonable objection to Copernicus, and how was it eventually resolved?
questions.answer: If Earth orbits the Sun, nearby stars should appear to shift back and forth over the year, yet no such shift was seen, which seemed to argue against a moving Earth. The resolution is that the shift is genuine but extremely small because the stars are enormously far away; once instruments became precise enough to measure it, parallax confirmed that Earth does move.
questions.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

questions.text: Describe the factor-label ('grid') method of unit conversion and why the units cancel.
questions.answer: You write the starting quantity and multiply it by a chain of conversion-factor fractions, each equal to one, arranged so that a unit in a numerator cancels the same unit in a denominator. By choosing the fractions so every unwanted unit cancels, you are left with only the desired units, and then you carry out the arithmetic on the numbers.

questions.answer: cancel

questions.answer: 11 km/s

questions.answer: False

questions.text: Use the video's free-fall idea to explain the difference between mass and weight.
questions.answer: Mass is the amount of matter in you and stays the same whether you are on Earth or in orbit. Weight is the push of a surface back against you; in orbit you are in continuous free fall with nothing pushing back, so you have no weight even though your mass — and Earth's gravity on you — is essentially unchanged.
questions.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

questions.answer: Match

questions.text: List the rules for determining significant figures.
questions.answer: 1) Leading zeros are never significant. 2) Trailing zeros are significant only if there is a decimal point in the number. 3) Non-zero digits are always significant. 4) Zeros sandwiched between non-zero digits are always significant.

questions.text: Describe what happens to a hypothesis if experimental data do NOT support it, and what happens if data are consistent over many repeated experiments.
questions.answer: If the data do not support the hypothesis, the hypothesis is reworked/revised and retested. If data DO support it, the experiment is repeated; if results stay consistent over many repetitions, a theory can be developed.

questions.answer: see pairs

pairs.right: Changed intentionally to test the hypothesis
Changes in response to the independent variable
Run without an independent variable as a baseline

questions.text: Explain why astronomers need more than one unit (meters, kilometers, AU, light-years, parsecs) to measure sizes and distances in space.
questions.answer: Sizes and distances in space span an enormous range. Meters and kilometers suit objects from meteoroids up to stars; the AU is convenient within the solar system; but the AU becomes unwieldy beyond it, so light-years and parsecs are used for distances to stars and galaxies. Each unit keeps the numbers at a manageable scale for the distances involved.

questions.text: Starting from Earth, describe our place in the universe by listing the structures from smallest to largest.
questions.answer: Earth is one of 8 planets orbiting the Sun (the solar system). The Sun is one of several hundred billion stars in the Milky Way Galaxy. The Milky Way is part of the Local Group of galaxies, which belongs to the Local (Virgo) Supercluster, which is part of even larger systems that fill the visible universe.

questions.answer: see pairs

A recognizable star pattern within or across constellations
The path of the Sun through the sky

An apparent backward drift caused by Earth passing the planet
The daily rising and setting of planets
A planet falling toward the Sun

questions.answer: Retrograde motion of the planets

questions.answer: Ellipses with the Sun at one focus

Ellipses with the Sun at one focus

questions.answer: Four moons orbiting Jupiter

options: The Milky Way being made of stars

questions.text: Eratosthenes is famous for being the first to measure:
questions.answer: The circumference of Earth
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: According to Kepler's second law, a planet moves fastest when it is:
questions.answer: Closest to the Sun
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Newton's great unification showed that the force pulling an apple down is the same force that:
questions.answer: Holds the Moon in orbit around Earth
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)
options: Holds the Moon in orbit around Earth

questions.text: The celestial sphere accurately represents the true three-dimensional distances of the stars.
questions.answer: False

questions.text: Stars rise in the east and set in the west.
questions.answer: True

questions.answer: False

questions.answer: False

questions.answer: True
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Kepler discovered that planetary orbits are ellipses rather than perfect circles.
questions.answer: True
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: The point on the celestial sphere directly overhead an observer is called the __________.
questions.answer: zenith
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: The apparent yearly path of the Sun through the stars is called the __________.
questions.answer: ecliptic
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: The North Star, located near the north celestial pole, is named __________.
questions.answer: Polaris
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: The principle that one should not make more assumptions than necessary is known as __________ __________.
questions.answer: Occam's razor
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Ptolemy added small circles called __________ to his model to reproduce retrograde motion.
questions.answer: epicycles
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Tycho Brahe's assistant, who used his data to derive three laws of planetary motion, was __________ __________.
questions.answer: Johannes Kepler
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Explain how the appearance of the sky's daily motion differs for observers at the North Pole, the equator, and an intermediate latitude.
questions.answer: At the North Pole, stars circle the zenith and never rise or set. At the equator, the celestial poles sit on the horizon and stars rise straight up and set straight down. At an intermediate latitude, the north celestial pole is partway up (its altitude equals the observer's latitude), stars rise and set at an angle, and some stars near the pole are circumpolar (never setting) while others rise and set.
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Why did explaining retrograde motion eventually lead astronomers away from the geocentric model?
questions.answer: A simple Earth-centered model could not naturally account for planets appearing to move backward against the stars. Ptolemy needed increasingly complex epicycles to reproduce it. The heliocentric model explained retrograde motion simply, as the natural result of Earth and another planet passing each other, so by Occam's razor the simpler explanation won out.
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Summarize Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.
questions.answer: 1) Planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus. 2) A line from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times, so planets move faster when nearer the Sun. 3) The square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its semimajor axis, so planets farther from the Sun take longer to orbit.
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

questions.text: Describe two pieces of evidence Aristotle used to argue that Earth is spherical.
questions.answer: Aristotle pointed to the round shadow Earth casts on the Moon during a lunar eclipse, and to the way travelers moving south see new stars that had been hidden below the southern horizon. (He also noted that ships' hulls disappear over the horizon before their sail tops.)
questions.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)
