Recognizable star pattern like the Big Dipper

questions.text: Under Bode's Law, the predicted distance for a planet is found by taking the sequence (0, 3, 6, 12, ...), then:
questions.answer: Adding 4 and dividing by 10
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Bode's Law applies reliably to most known exoplanetary systems.
questions.answer: False — it fits our solar system reasonably well out to Uranus but does not reliably apply to many exoplanetary systems.
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: The formula for surface gravity is:
questions.answer: g = GM/R^2
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Earth's surface gravity is about ______ m/s^2.
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Two planets with the same radius but different masses will have the same surface gravity.
questions.answer: False — for equal radius, the planet with greater mass has the greater surface gravity (g = GM/R^2).
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Henry Cavendish first measured the gravitational constant G in the year ______.
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: The approximate value of the gravitational constant G is:
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: At what approximate altitude does a satellite achieve a geosynchronous orbit?
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: A geostationary orbit is a geosynchronous orbit located over the equator, keeping the satellite above the same point on Earth.
questions.answer: True
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Orbits with altitudes below about 2000 km, used by the ISS and Hubble, are called ______ Earth orbit.
questions.answer: low
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: A planet's average density is calculated by:
questions.answer: Dividing its mass by its volume
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)
options: Dividing its mass by its volume

questions.text: Explain how astronomers determine a planet's average density, naming the source of each quantity.
questions.answer: Average density is mass divided by volume (rho = M/V). The mass is found from Kepler's modified third law (M = 4*pi^2*d^3 / (G*P^2)) by observing the distance and period of a satellite orbiting the planet. The volume is found from the planet's measured radius (V = 4/3*pi*R^3), with the radius obtained from the planet's angular size and distance. Dividing the two gives the average density, a key clue to interior composition.
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

questions.text: Match each Earth-orbit class to its defining altitude or feature.
questions.answer: LEO -> below ~2000 km; MEO -> ~2000 to 35,786 km; Geosynchronous -> one orbit per day; High Earth orbit -> above ~35,786 km
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)
pairs: Below about 2000 km; ISS and Hubble
About 2000 to 35,786 km; navigation and communication
About 35,786 km; one orbit per day
Above about 35,786 km; studies space such as the Sun

questions.text: Match each gravity concept to its description.
questions.answer: Surface gravity -> g=GM/R^2; Gravitational constant -> 6.67e-11; Cavendish -> measured G in 1798; Net force -> overall combined force
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)
pairs: Acceleration at a body's surface, g = GM/R^2
The overall force left after combining all forces

questions.text: Why does surface gravity influence whether a celestial object can retain an atmosphere?
questions.answer: Surface gravity sets how strongly a body holds onto gas molecules. A body with higher mass (and thus higher surface gravity for a given radius) produces a higher escape velocity, making it harder for gas molecules to reach escape speed and drift away. Low-gravity bodies like the Moon let gas escape easily and end up with little or no atmosphere, while higher-gravity bodies like Earth retain one.
questions.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

notes.title: Ch. 3 Lecture — Gravity and Motion (slides: Bode's Law, surface gravity, satellites)
notes.body:
# Ch. 3 Lecture — Gravity and Motion (lecture slides)

NOTE: Supplements the OpenStax Ch. 3 note (Orbits and Gravity). This note emphasizes the lecture deck's added material — Bode's Law, surface gravity, the gravitational constant, satellite orbits, and how mass/density/composition are measured. Core Kepler/Newton/escape-velocity content lives in the book note.

## Bode's Law (Titius–Bode Rule)
- First noted 1766; formalized by J. E. Bode in 1778.
- Rule: take 0, 3, 6, 12, 24, ... (double after 3), add 4, divide by 10 -> distance in AU.
- Fits reasonably well Mercury -> Uranus; misses Neptune (predicts 38.8 vs true 30.1) and Pluto.
- Does NOT reliably apply to many exoplanetary systems.

## The Law of Gravity, refined
- Every mass attracts every other mass: F_G = G(M*m)/d^2.
- **G = 6.67 x 10^-11 N·m^2/kg^2** — measured by **Cavendish (1798)** with a torsion balance.
- G is hard to measure because gravity between lab masses is extremely weak.

## Surface gravity
- From Newton's 2nd law + law of gravity: m*a = GMm/R^2  ->  **g = GM/R^2**.
- **g(Earth) = 9.8 m/s^2.**
- Same radius, more mass -> larger surface gravity.
- Surface gravity sets weight, influences a body's shape, and affects atmosphere retention.

## Measuring an object's mass from orbits
- Kepler's modified 3rd law: **M = 4*pi^2*d^3 / (G*P^2)**.
- Observe a small satellite's distance d and period P -> solve for the large body's mass M.

## Measuring composition
- **Reflection spectrum** -> atmospheric contents + surface rock type.
- **Seismic activity** -> interior structure (only measured on Earth so far).
- **Average density** = M / V:
  - M from Kepler's modified 3rd law (satellite orbit).
  - V = 4/3*pi*R^3 from measured radius (via angular size + distance).
- Using density: compare candidate material densities, account for gravitational compression, surface spectra, density differentiation, and equatorial-bulge analysis.

## Satellites and orbit types
- A **stable orbit** needs the right separation and the correct velocity; too fast -> flung away, too slow -> falls in.
- **Low Earth Orbit (LEO):** < ~2000 km — ISS, Hubble, observation sats.
- **Medium Earth Orbit (MEO):** ~2000–35,786 km — navigation, communication, science.
- **Geosynchronous:** ~35,786 km — one orbit per day.
- **Geostationary:** geosynchronous AND over the equator — stays above one point.
- **High Earth Orbit:** > ~35,786 km — studies space (Sun, solar wind).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Bode's Law: (sequence + 4) / 10 = distance in AU; fails past Uranus and for exoplanets.
- ★ **g = GM/R^2**; g(Earth) = 9.8 m/s^2; more mass (same R) -> more surface gravity.
- ★ **G = 6.67 x 10^-11**, measured by **Cavendish, 1798**.
- ★ Mass from orbit: **M = 4*pi^2*d^3 / (G*P^2)**.
- ★ Density = M/V -> clue to interior composition.
- ★ Geosynchronous = 35,786 km / one day; geostationary = geosynchronous + over equator.
notes.source: Astronomy lecture slides, Ch. 3: Gravity and Motion (McGraw-Hill Education)

notes.title: Chapter 2 (Part 2) - The Birth of Astronomy
notes.body:
CHAPTER 2 (PART 2) - THE BIRTH OF ASTRONOMY

== GEOCENTRIC MODELS ==
- The general east-to-west motion of the sky prompted Earth-centered (geocentric) models.
- Eudoxus (~400-347 BC): each object rode its own revolving transparent sphere with its own tilt; faster objects had smaller spheres.
- A simple geocentric model could NOT explain retrograde motion.
- Ptolemy of Alexandria: added EPICYCLES - a planet moves on a small circle whose center moves on a larger circle around Earth - to reproduce retrograde motion. His model predicted planetary positions with fair precision, was published in a star catalogue (140 CE), and remained canon for centuries (spreading to Arabic and European astronomers).
- Eventually geocentric models collapsed under OCCAM'S RAZOR ('entities should not be multiplied without necessity' - don't make more assumptions than needed), and heliocentric models prevailed.

== ANCIENT & NON-WESTERN ASTRONOMERS ==
- Mayans (~750 BC): tracked the skies and made the first almanacs (tables predicting Sun/Moon/planet motions).
- Thales of Miletus (585 BC): correctly predicted a solar eclipse.
- Pythagoras (~500 BC): taught that Earth is round (the sphere as the 'perfect' shape).
- Babylonians (~400 BC): devised the zodiac system, dividing the sky into 12 equal segments.
- Plato (387 BC): promoted the geocentric model with perfect circular orbits.
- Aristotle (~300 BC): gave naked-eye evidence for a spherical Earth - Earth's curved shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse, new stars appearing as you travel south, and ships' hulls vanishing before their sails.
- Aristarchus (~a century after Aristotle): estimated relative sizes/distances of the Sun and Moon (the best for ~2000 years) using angular size and geometry; proposed a heliocentric model, but the lack of observable parallax argued against it. (Parallax = the apparent shift of a foreground object relative to the background.)
- Eratosthenes (276-195 BC): made the first measurement of Earth's circumference (~25,000 miles, close to today's value) from shadow lengths at Alexandria vs. Syene on the summer solstice.
- Islamic astronomers: preserved/expanded ancient texts through Europe's Middle Ages, set religious calendars by the sky, gave us vocabulary (zenith, Betelgeuse), built the astrolabe, questioned Ptolemy, and developed algebra and Arabic numerals.
- Asian astronomers: created mythology-based constellations, kept detailed records of eclipses, comets, supernovae, and sunspots, and predicted eclipses.

== THE RENAISSANCE ==
- Copernicus (1473-1543): couldn't reconcile data with Ptolemy, so revived Aristarchus's heliocentric model. Heliocentrism explains retrograde motion naturally (two planets passing) and gives the planets' relative distances and why Venus/Mercury stay near the Sun. In 1543 he published 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres' (Earth rotates on its axis; planet order). LIMITATIONS: still used circular orbits and 8 spheres, predicted no better than Ptolemy, couldn't explain the missing stellar parallax, and clashed with Aristotelian 'common sense.' Still, it sparked the Scientific Revolution.
- Tycho Brahe (1546-1601): built instruments of unmatched accuracy and made meticulous planetary measurements; observed a supernova and a comet (showing the heavens change); but seeing no parallax, he proposed a compromise geocentric model.
- Johannes Kepler (1571-1630): inherited Tycho's superb data and derived three laws of planetary motion; his key fix was ELLIPTICAL (not circular) orbits.
   * 1st law: elliptical orbits, Sun at one focus.
   * 2nd law: equal areas in equal times (faster near the Sun).
   * 3rd law: P squared is proportional to a cubed (period vs. semimajor axis); hints at the force holding planets in orbit.
   * Eccentricity describes how stretched an ellipse is (closer to 1 = flatter).
- Galileo (1564-1642): first to turn a telescope on the heavens and interpret what he saw - sunspots, lunar craters, four moons of Jupiter, and the Milky Way as countless stars. Jupiter's moons proved not everything orbits Earth, undercutting geocentrism. A founder of modern science (experimental method, first correct laws of motion). The Roman Inquisition tried him in 1633, found him 'vehemently suspect of heresy,' and kept him under house arrest until his death in 1642.
- Isaac Newton (1642-1727): born the year Galileo died; published the three laws of motion and the theory of gravity in 1687. He unified the force that makes an apple fall with the force that keeps the Moon in orbit (gravity), pioneered motion/optics/gravity, and invented calculus. His laws stood until Einstein's relativity in the 20th century.

== NEW DISCOVERIES & TECHNOLOGIES ==
- William Herschel (1781): discovered Uranus and that stars can have companions; orbital irregularities in Uranus later led to Neptune's discovery.
- Better optics -> larger telescopes -> nebulae and galaxies; photography revealed faint objects; spectroscopy revealed stars' composition; Edwin Hubble confirmed galaxies beyond the Milky Way.
- 20th-21st centuries: Pluto's discovery, human spaceflight and Moon landings, satellites and probes, the ISS, Mars rovers, exoplanet detection and habitability studies, the first black-hole photograph, and Perseverance on Mars.

=========================================
★ KEY EXAM POINTS ★
- Geocentric (Earth-centered) needed EPICYCLES; heliocentric (Sun-centered) explains retrograde simply - Occam's razor favors it.
- Eratosthenes = first to measure Earth's size; Aristarchus = early heliocentric proposal (defeated by no parallax).
- Sequence of the revolution: Copernicus (heliocentric, 1543) -> Tycho (data) -> Kepler (elliptical orbits + 3 laws) -> Galileo (telescope, Jupiter's moons) -> Newton (gravity + motion, 1687).
- Kepler's 3rd law: P^2 is proportional to a^3.
- Galileo's moons of Jupiter = objects NOT orbiting Earth = geocentric model is wrong.
- Newton unified terrestrial and celestial motion under one gravity; later refined by Einstein.
notes.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

notes.title: Chapter 2 (Part 1) - The Cycles of the Sky
notes.body:
CHAPTER 2 (PART 1) - THE CYCLES OF THE SKY

== THE CELESTIAL SPHERE ==
- Vast distances hide the stars' true 3-D arrangement, so to the naked eye all stars appear equally distant on a giant celestial sphere with Earth at the center.
- The celestial sphere is a MODEL - it doesn't have to match physical reality. Models exist to enhance our understanding of nature.
- Ancient Greeks imagined a crystalline geocentric (Earth-centered) sphere carrying the stars. The model is incorrect, but still handy for visualizing and mapping the sky (think planetarium); object positions appear fixed relative to one another.

== THE SKY AROUND US ==
- Horizon = where the sky meets the ground. Zenith = the point directly overhead.
- Stars rise in the EAST and set in the WEST.
- The sky appears to turn, but really EARTH turns - the celestial sphere stays still.

== CELESTIAL POLES, EQUATOR & COORDINATES ==
- Extending Earth's rotational axis to the sphere gives the north and south celestial poles. Polaris (the North Star) sits near the north celestial pole - 'the fastener of the sky.'
- The celestial equator lies directly above Earth's equator and serves as a reference marker.
- What you see depends on your LATITUDE:
   (a) At a pole: stars circle a fixed point and never rise or set.
   (b) At the equator: the celestial poles sit on the horizon; stars rise straight up and set straight down.
   (c) At an intermediate latitude: the north celestial pole's altitude EQUALS your latitude; stars rise and set at an angle, and some are circumpolar.
- Right ascension (RA) is like longitude (east/west of the vernal equinox). Declination (Dec) is like latitude (angle above/below the celestial equator).

== CONSTELLATIONS & ASTERISMS ==
- Every culture built sky stories; astronomy is one of the oldest sciences. Many familiar figures come from ancient Greece.
- Modern constellation = a PATCH (sector) of sky that carries a name, not just the stars. The IAU defined 88 official constellations in 1928; 48 are ancient.
- An asterism is a SUBSET of stars forming a recognizable shape: the Big Dipper (within Ursa Major) and Orion's Belt (within Orion) are classic examples.
- In Orion: Betelgeuse is the bright red armpit star; Rigel is the bright blue foot star.
- Star positions change very slowly, so constellations look the same for thousands of years; their ancient origins likely served season-tracking and navigation.

== DAILY & ANNUAL MOTION ==
- Daily motion (east-to-west rising/setting) comes from Earth's rotation about the celestial poles.
- Annual motion: a given star rises about 3 minutes 56 seconds EARLIER each night, caused by Earth's orbit around the Sun. At a fixed clock time, constellations shift across the year - used by the ancients to mark seasons.

== THE ECLIPTIC & ZODIAC ==
- The ecliptic is the Sun's apparent path through the stars - the projection of Earth's orbit, tipped ~23.5 degrees to the celestial equator.
- The zodiac is the belt around the ecliptic, passing through ~12 constellations (a 13th, Ophiuchus, also crosses it). The Sun, Moon, and planets all travel within it - the basis of astrology.

== PLANETS & RETROGRADE MOTION ==
- 'Planet' is Greek for 'wanderer.' Planets drift relative to the stars within the narrow zodiac band; their motion combines orbits nearly in one plane with differing speeds.
- Usually planets drift west-to-east relative to the stars (though daily they still rise in the east).
- Retrograde motion = occasional east-to-west drift relative to the stars. It is APPARENT, not real: the planet doesn't reverse its orbit; it just looks that way as Earth and the planet pass each other.
- Explaining retrograde motion was a key reason astronomers eventually rejected the Earth-centered solar system.

=========================================
★ KEY EXAM POINTS ★
- Celestial sphere = a MODEL; Earth rotates, the sphere doesn't.
- Horizon (sky meets ground) vs. zenith (straight overhead).
- Altitude of the north celestial pole = your latitude.
- RA ~ longitude; Dec ~ latitude.
- Constellation = named REGION of sky (88 total, IAU 1928); asterism = star PATTERN (Big Dipper, Orion's Belt).
- Ecliptic = Sun's path, tilted ~23.5 degrees; zodiac = ~12 constellations along it.
- Retrograde motion is APPARENT, caused by Earth overtaking/being overtaken by a planet.
notes.source: Cycles of the Sky & Birth of Astronomy lecture slides (Ch 2)

notes.title: Chapter 2 - The Size & Structure of Space
notes.body:
CHAPTER 2 - THE SIZE & STRUCTURE OF SPACE

== OUR PLACE IN THE UNIVERSE (smallest -> largest) ==
- Earth is 1 of 8 planets orbiting the Sun.
- The Sun is 1 of several hundred billion stars in the Milky Way Galaxy.
- The Milky Way + 2 similar galaxies + dozens of smaller ones = the Local Group.
- The Local Group is part of the Local (Virgo) Supercluster, part of even larger systems filling the visible universe.
- Despite all the stars and galaxies, MOST of space is empty.

== MATTER & ATOMS (the very small) ==
- Matter is made of atoms: electrons surrounding a nucleus.
- The nucleus = protons (+) and neutrons (neutral), densely packed; orbitals = electron clouds held by proton charge.
- An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains its properties; atoms bond into molecules and compounds.
- Typical atom size ~10^-10 m (= 1 angstrom = 0.1 nm).
- Atomic number = # protons = # electrons (identifies the element).
- Atomic mass = # protons + # neutrons (in amu); proton and neutron = 1 amu each; electron ~ 0.
  - Example (periodic table): Carbon, atomic number 6, symbol C, atomic mass 12.0.
- The universe gets its structure from the electromagnetic force, the strong and weak forces (atomic scale), and gravity.

== UNITS FOR SIZES IN SPACE ==
- Meter: meteoroids, small asteroids/comets (e.g., a 25 m asteroid).
- Kilometer (1 km = 1000 m): asteroids, comets, moons, dwarf planets, planets, stars (e.g., Rigel radius ~55 million km).
- AU: average Earth-Sun distance ~150 million km = 1.496 x 10^8 km. Earth is closest to the Sun in January, farthest in July.
  - Example: Jupiter is 778,500,000 km = 5.2 AU from the Sun (5.2x Earth's distance).
  - The AU stops being useful beyond the solar system (Proxima Centauri = 265,613 AU; Andromeda = 1.6 x 10^11 AU).
- Light-year: 1 ly = 9.461 x 10^12 km = 63,241.1 AU. Proxima Centauri is 4.2 ly away (light takes 4.2 yr to travel there).
- Parsec: defined via parallax (a 1 arcsecond angle as Earth orbits); 1 pc = 3.26 ly. GN-z11, one of the farthest/oldest galaxies, is ~9.8 billion pc (31.9 billion ly) away.
- Quick reference: 1 AU ~93 million miles; light speed ~300 million m/s (186,000 mi/s); 1 ly ~6 trillion miles (~10 trillion km).

== A TOUR OF THE SOLAR SYSTEM ==
- Order out from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, asteroid belt, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune, Kuiper belt, Oort cloud.
- Moon: ~250,000 miles away; ~1/4 Earth's diameter; ~1/80 Earth's mass; airless, pitted rock.
- Planet highlights: Mercury (cratered, airless); Venus (sulfuric-acid clouds); Earth (oceans, life); Mars (canyons, possible ancient water); Jupiter (giant storms; Great Red Spot ~Earth-sized); Saturn (icy ring fragments; 2nd largest); Uranus (dark rings, tipped spin); Neptune (deep blue, methane clouds).
- The Sun: ball of gas (mostly H and He); ~100x Earth's diameter; ~300x more massive; fuses H -> He; ~5 billion years old (about halfway through its life).

== THE MILKY WAY & BEYOND ==
- Milky Way: several hundred billion stars, ~100,000 ly across; Sun ~27,000 ly from center, orbiting at 150 mi/s (~210 million yr per orbit).
- Nearest star: Proxima Centauri, ~25 trillion miles (~4.2 ly); essentially empty space between.
- Galaxy clusters: Milky Way is in the Local Group (a few dozen galaxies, a few million ly across) -> Virgo Supercluster (hundreds of groups, ~100 million ly) -> Great Attractor region (>300 million ly).
- The visible universe is the largest known structure, ~13.8 billion years old (how far we can see).

== THE STILL-UNKNOWN UNIVERSE ==
- Most of the universe is NOT planets, stars, or galaxies.
- Dark matter: emits no observed radiation; outweighs luminous matter ~5:1; may interact only via gravity and the weak force.
- The Big Bang (~13.8 billion yr ago) created time and space and started cosmic expansion; the expansion is ACCELERATING.
- Dark energy is the name for whatever drives that accelerating expansion.
- Approximate composition: dark energy ~69%, dark matter ~25%, atomic matter ~5% (plus tiny fractions of neutrinos, photons, black holes).

=========================================
★ KEY EXAM POINTS ★
- Order of scale: Earth -> Solar System -> Milky Way -> Local Group -> Supercluster -> visible universe.
- Atomic number = protons = electrons; atomic mass = protons + neutrons (amu).
- Match the unit to the distance: km (solar-system bodies), AU (within solar system), ly & pc (stars/galaxies).
- 1 AU ~150 million km; 1 ly ~9.46 x 10^12 km; 1 pc = 3.26 ly.
- Sun fuses H into He; ~5 billion yr old, halfway through its life.
- Universe ~13.8 billion yr old; composition ~69% dark energy, ~25% dark matter, ~5% ordinary matter.
- Dark matter outweighs luminous matter ~5:1; dark energy drives accelerating expansion.

notes.title: Chapter 1 - Intro to Astronomy & the Nature of Science
notes.body:
CHAPTER 1 - INTRODUCTION TO ASTRONOMY & THE NATURE OF SCIENCE

== THE NATURE OF ASTRONOMY ==
- Astronomy is the study of objects beyond our planet and the processes by which they interact.
- The universe is DYNAMIC, not static.
- Tracing the universe's evolution and predicting its future are central to modern astronomy.

== THE NATURE OF SCIENCE ==
- Science is both a BODY OF KNOWLEDGE and a METHOD for understanding, organizing, and communicating it.
- Discovery begins with curiosity: observe a phenomenon and ask questions.

== THE SCIENTIFIC METHOD (steps) ==
1. Make observations
   - Observation = information obtained through the senses.
   - Inference = a conclusion drawn from observations.
2. Develop a question or problem.
3. Form a hypothesis (a proposed answer; must be TESTABLE).
4. Test the hypothesis:
   - Independent variable = changed intentionally (manipulated).
   - Dependent variable = changes in response (responding).
   - Controlled variable(s) = kept constant.
   - Control group = a setup run without the independent variable.
5. Analyze data and draw conclusions:
   - Not supported -> rework the hypothesis and retest.
   - Supported -> repeat the experiment.
   - Consistent over many repetitions -> a theory can develop.

== TYPES OF DATA ==
- Qualitative: descriptions; observed but not measured (color, texture, smell, appearance). Qualitative -> QUALITY.
- Quantitative: numbers; measured (length, volume, weight, speed, time, temperature). Quantitative -> QUANTITY.

== THEORIES & LAWS ==
- Scientific theory = a well-tested EXPLANATION for a set of observations.
- Scientific law = a statement that SUMMARIZES a pattern in nature; it describes but does not explain.
- A theory is never 'proven' - data can support it, but nothing is proven ('we don't know what we don't know').

== NUMBERS IN ASTRONOMY ==
- Numbers in astronomy are very large, so scientific notation is used: a number between 1 and 10 x a power of ten.
  - 1,000,000 = 1 x 10^6;  1,000,000,000,000 = 1 x 10^12
- New units are invented when standard ones are unwieldy:
  - Light-year (ly) = distance light travels in one year = 9.46 x 10^12 km.
  - Astronomical Unit (AU) = average Earth-Sun distance = 1.496 x 10^8 km.
  - Parsec (pc) = 3.262 ly = 3.086 x 10^12 km.

== SIGNIFICANT FIGURES ==
Rules: 1) Leading zeros are never significant. 2) Trailing zeros count only with a decimal point present. 3) Non-zero digits are always significant. 4) Zeros sandwiched between non-zeros are always significant.
In equations:
  - Add/Subtract -> match the fewest DECIMAL places (7.42 + 3.5 = 10.9).
  - Multiply/Divide -> match the fewest SIG FIGS (100 x 53 = 5000; 100.0 x 53 = 5,300).

=========================================
★ KEY EXAM POINTS ★
- Observation (senses) vs. inference (conclusion from observations).
- Independent = manipulated; dependent = responding; control group has NO independent variable.
- Theory EXPLAINS; law DESCRIBES. Nothing in science is ever 'proven.'
- Scientific notation format: 1-10 x power of ten.
- Sig figs: leading zeros never count; trailing zeros need a decimal; +/- match decimals, x/divide match sig figs.
- Memorize: 1 ly = 9.46 x 10^12 km; 1 AU = 1.496 x 10^8 km; 1 pc = 3.26 ly.

notes.title: Video 7 — The Gravity of the Situation
notes.body:
# Video 7 — The Gravity of the Situation

## Mass vs density vs weight
- **Mass:** how much 'stuff' is in an object; also its resistance to changes in motion. Same everywhere.
- **Density:** mass per volume (same mass + bigger size = lower density).
- **Weight:** how hard a surface pushes back on you; it's zero in free fall.

## How gravity works
- Everything with mass has gravity; force depends on **both masses and the distance**.
- **Inverse-square law:** double the distance -> force ÷ 4; ten times farther -> force ÷ 100.
- Gravity is **attractive only**.

## Orbits = falling and missing the ground
- Throw a rock sideways hard enough and it falls *around* Earth -> an **orbit**.
- Orbit shapes by increasing speed: **line -> circle -> ellipse -> parabola -> hyperbola**.
- Low-Earth circular orbit takes ~90 minutes.

## Escape velocity
- Throw hard enough and gravity weakens faster than it can stop you -> the object **escapes**.
- **Earth ≈ 11 km/s, Jupiter ≈ 58 km/s, Sun ≈ 600 km/s.**
- Escape path = parabola; faster than escape = hyperbola.

## Why astronauts are 'weightless'
- Not because gravity is gone — at the ISS, Earth's gravity is still ~**90%** of surface value.
- They're in **free fall** (orbiting), so nothing pushes back -> no weight. NASA calls it **microgravity**.
- Same **mass** as on Earth, but no **weight**.

## Bonus: gravity warps space
- Even massless **photons** bend near massive objects -> gravity curves space itself (preview of black holes).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Inverse-square: 2× distance -> 1/4 force; 10× -> 1/100.
- ★ Orbit shapes by speed: line, circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.
- ★ Escape velocity: Earth ~11, Jupiter ~58, Sun ~600 km/s.
- ★ Astronauts are weightless from **free fall**, not absence of gravity (mass stays; weight = 0).
notes.source: Course Video 7: The Gravity of the Situation (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 6 — Kepler's Laws and Beyond
notes.body:
# Video 6 — Kepler's Laws and Beyond

## Brahe -> Kepler
- **Tycho Brahe** built the best instruments of his era and gathered the best pre-telescope data.
- His assistant **Johannes Kepler** analyzed it and found planets trace **ellipses**, not circles.

## Ellipse basics
- Two **foci**; the sum of distances from the foci to any point is constant.
- A circle is an ellipse whose foci coincide; planet orbits have **low eccentricity**, so they look nearly circular.

## Kepler's three laws
- **1st:** each orbit is an **ellipse with the Sun at one focus** (closest point = **perihelion**, farthest = **aphelion**).
- **2nd:** a planet sweeps **equal areas in equal times** -> faster near the Sun, slower far away.
- **3rd:** **P² ∝ a³** (period in years, semimajor axis in AU).
- Laws are **empirical** yet extremely precise -> the universe obeys decipherable math (a foundation of modern science).

## Galileo (telescope, same era)
- Moon has mountains/craters; **sunspots** that move -> Sun rotates.
- **Four moons of Jupiter** -> not everything orbits Earth.
- Saturn's rings; **phases of Venus** -> 'final nail' for geocentrism; far more stars than the eye sees.

## Newton
- Born the year Galileo died.
- Showed **falling and orbiting have the same cause**; Kepler's three laws follow from Newton's laws of motion + universal gravitation.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Brahe = data; Kepler = ellipses (orbits look circular only because eccentricity is low).
- ★ Kepler: 1) ellipse, Sun at focus; 2) equal areas/equal times; 3) P² ∝ a³.
- ★ Galileo's **phases of Venus** = decisive against geocentrism.
- ★ Newton unified Kepler's laws via motion + gravity.
notes.source: Course Video 6: History of Astronomy IV: Kepler's Laws and Beyond (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 5 — Simple Unit Conversion (the 'grid' method)
notes.body:
# Video 5 — Simple Unit Conversion

## The method: factor-label / 'grid' / 'train-track'
1. Write down your **conversion factors** (e.g., 1 mi = 1.6 km, 1 hr = 60 min, 1 min = 60 s).
2. Start with the given quantity, then multiply by factor-fractions arranged so the **unwanted units cancel** diagonally.
3. Keep chaining until only the desired units remain, then multiply/divide the numbers.

## Worked example A: 70 mph -> km/s
- 70 mi/hr × (1.6 km / 1 mi) × (1 hr / 60 min) × (1 min / 60 s)
- mi, hr, min all cancel -> km/s.
- 70 × 1.6 ÷ 60 ÷ 60 ≈ **0.03 km/s = 3 × 10⁻² km/s**.

## Worked example B: 70 km/s -> mph
- 70 km/s × (1 mi / 1.6 km) × (60 s / 1 min) × (60 min / 1 hr)
- ≈ **156,586 mph ≈ 1.57 × 10⁵ mph** (very fast!).
- Sanity check: 70 km ≈ 42 mi, so 70 km/s ≈ 42 mi/s — a huge distance each second.

## Scientific notation
- Move the decimal so one nonzero digit is in front; the exponent counts the moves.
- 0.03 -> **3 × 10⁻²**;  157,000 -> **1.57 × 10⁵**.
- Why bother: it tames very large/small numbers (no long strings of zeros).

## Instructor's advice
- Just practice — do the study-guide conversion problems, then invent your own.
- You already do conversions intuitively (1 yard = 3 ft, 1 ft = 12 in, 1 m = 100 cm).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Set up factor-label fractions so **units cancel**, leaving the target units.
- ★ 70 mph ≈ **0.03 km/s** (3 × 10⁻²); 70 km/s ≈ **1.57 × 10⁵ mph**.
- ★ Scientific notation: one digit before the decimal × 10^(places moved).
notes.source: Course Video 5: Simple Unit Conversion (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 4 — Copernicus and Heliocentrism
notes.body:
# Video 4 — Copernicus and Heliocentrism

## Ptolemy's geocentric model
- Perfected the Earth-centered model (2nd-century Egypt).
- Explained **retrograde motion** with **epicycles** (small circles on the main orbit).
- Reasonably accurate but messy: a different formula per planet; tinkered with for centuries until the 1500s.

## Copernicus
- Revived the **heliocentric** (Sun-centered) model.
- Problems of geocentrism vanish: inner planets orbit faster; **retrograde = Earth overtaking an outer planet** (like passing a slower car on the highway).
- Even estimated planet-Sun distances with clever geometry.

## The parallax objection (a valid one)
- If Earth moves, nearby stars should appear to **shift** (parallax).
- It's real but **tiny** because stars are so far; not measurable until centuries later.
- **Finger/eye analogy:** blink each eye and a near finger shifts; the shift shrinks with distance.
- Method: Earth's two orbital positions (baseline = **1 AU ≈ 150 million km**); half the shift = the star's **parallax**; trig gives the distance. Parallax confirms heliocentrism.

## Cultural impact
- If Earth orbits the Sun, Earth is **not** the center of the universe.
- **Giordano Bruno** argued stars are other suns with possible worlds/life; the Church tried him for heresy and burned him at the stake.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Geocentrism used **epicycles** for retrograde; heliocentrism explains it as Earth overtaking outer planets.
- ★ **Parallax** = apparent star shift from Earth's orbit; tiny because of huge distances; confirms heliocentrism.
- ★ Parallax baseline = **1 AU**; half the measured shift = the parallax angle.
notes.source: Course Video 4: History of Astronomy III: Copernicus and Heliocentrism (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 3 — Early Measurements of the Earth
notes.body:
# Video 3 — Early Measurements of the Earth

## Science = observation + what we do with it
- Observe -> build models -> predict -> measure -> revise if they don't match.

## Earth is round
- **Pythagoras:** proposed it on aesthetic grounds (the sphere is 'perfect') — not yet scientific.
- **Aristotle (logical evidence):**
  - Earth's **curved shadow** on the Moon during lunar eclipses.
  - **Different stars** become visible as you travel north-south.

## Eratosthenes measures Earth's circumference
- Sun directly overhead a **well at Syene** at noon on the summer solstice.
- Same moment, an **obelisk at Alexandria** cast a shadow showing the Sun **7° off vertical**.
- 7° ≈ **1/50** of a full circle.
- Distance between cities = 5,000 stadia -> circumference ≈ 50 × 5,000 = **250,000 stadia ≈ 25,000 miles** (remarkably accurate).

## Aristarchus
- From the curvature of Earth's shadow on the Moon: Moon's diameter ≈ **1/3 of Earth's**.
- Argued the **Sun is much larger than Earth**, and even proposed a **Sun-centered** model (too early to be accepted).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Aristotle's evidence for a round Earth: curved eclipse shadow + changing stars with latitude.
- ★ Eratosthenes: 7° ≈ 1/50 circle -> circumference ≈ 50 × (city distance) ≈ 25,000 miles.
- ★ Aristarchus: Moon ≈ 1/3 Earth's diameter; first to propose heliocentrism.
notes.source: Course Video 3: History of Astronomy II: Early Measurements of the Earth (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 2 — The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations
notes.body:
# Video 2 — The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations

## The celestial sphere
- Ancients saw thousands of 'fixed' stars on a slowly rotating dome.
- They grouped them into **constellations**; the Sun, Moon, and 5 planets moved separately.
- Planets crossed special constellations = the **zodiac** (still used in astrology).

## Sky cycles -> units of time
- **Day** = Sun rises/sets (most obvious cycle).
- **Month** = Moon's phases.
- **Year** = seasons.

## The north celestial pole
- One point doesn't move: the **NCP** (above Earth's rotation axis).
- The **north star** sits there -> useful for navigation; a point with no lateral motion as Earth spins.

## Why the sky changes through the year
- As Earth orbits, the **Sun blocks different background stars**, so different constellations are visible at night across the year.

## The ecliptic
- The Sun's path across the sky = the **plane of the solar system** (planets follow it too).
- Named because **eclipses** can happen when the Moon crosses it.

## Seasons = AXIAL TILT (not distance!)
- Axis tilted **23.5°**; opposite hemispheres have opposite seasons, so distance can't be the cause.
- Hemisphere tilted toward Sun -> more direct light + longer days = summer.
- **Equinoxes:** Sun crosses the celestial equator (equal day/night).
- **Solstices:** Sun farthest from the celestial equator.
- Monuments like **Stonehenge** frame solstice/equinox sunrises — careful observation, not aliens.

## Moon phases (NOT shadows)
- We see only the sunlit face; how much of it points toward Earth gives the phase.
- Full = Moon opposite Sun; New = Moon on Sun's side; crescents in between.
- **Solar eclipse:** Moon between Sun and Earth. **Lunar eclipse:** Earth between Sun and Moon.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Seasons = **23.5° axial tilt**, NOT Earth-Sun distance.
- ★ Lunar phases come from geometry of the sunlit face — **not** Earth's shadow.
- ★ Ecliptic = Sun's path = plane of the solar system.
- ★ Different stars each season because the Sun blocks different background stars.
notes.source: Course Video 2: History of Astronomy I: The Celestial Sphere & Early Observations (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Video 1 — Introduction to Astronomy
notes.body:
# Video 1 — Introduction to Astronomy

## What science is
- A body of knowledge AND a method for building it.
- Core requirement: **honesty** — actively looking for ways our ideas might be wrong.
- Science self-corrects: it gets ever closer to reality over time.

## What astronomy is
- The study of objects and phenomena beyond Earth.
- Boundaries with other sciences are fuzzy (a Mars rover does geology/chemistry too).
- "Astronomy puts you in your place": a scale tour from Earth -> Sun -> Milky Way -> Local Group -> Virgo Supercluster -> observable universe.

## Astronomy vs astrology
- **Astronomy = science** (evidence-based).
- **Astrology = not science**: no real predictive power; people remember the hits and forget the misses.
- Historically astrology motivated careful sky-watching, which seeded real astronomy.

## Who "does" astronomy
- Not just telescope operators: astrophysicists, engineers, technicians, **programmers**, teachers, writers, even artists.

## Quick history (revolutions)
- Geocentrism -> Copernicus (heliocentric) -> Brahe/Kepler -> Newton (calculus + gravity) -> Galileo & telescopes -> photography -> digital detectors -> space telescopes.

## Memorable facts from the video
- Earth ~13,000 km across; Sun ~150 million km away.
- Observable universe ~90 billion light-years across, expanding (dark energy).
- We can directly see only ~**4%** of the universe.
- Exploding stars forged the elements in you (iron in blood, calcium in bones, phosphorus in DNA).
- The most common star type is too faint to see without a telescope (red dwarfs).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Astronomy = science; astrology = not (no predictive validity).
- ★ Science's defining trait per the lecture: **honesty / self-correction**.
- ★ Revolution order: Copernicus -> Kepler -> Newton -> telescopes -> photography -> digital -> space.
- ★ Elements in our bodies were forged in stars.
notes.source: Course Video 1: Introduction to Astronomy (Week 1 lecture)

notes.title: Ch. 29 — The Big Bang
notes.body:
# The Big Bang

## What cosmology must explain
- The **expansion** (Hubble's law), the **cosmic microwave background**, the **light-element abundances**, and large-scale **uniformity + structure**.

## Timeline highlights
- Hot, dense start ~**13.8 billion years** ago.
- First minutes: **Big Bang nucleosynthesis** -> ~25% helium (nuclei form).
- ~**380,000 yr**: **recombination** -> neutral atoms form -> releases the **CMB**.
- (So **nuclei form before atoms**.)

## Key successes
- Predicts the **CMB** and the primordial **helium** abundance.

## Beyond the basic model
- **Inflation** (brief rapid early expansion) solves the **flatness** and **horizon** problems.
- **Dark matter:** unseen mass (galaxy rotation, cluster gravity).
- **Dark energy:** drives the **accelerating** expansion (from distant **Type Ia supernovae**).
- **Cosmological redshift:** expanding space stretches light -> all distant galaxies redshifted.
- **Anthropic principle:** the universe's properties are compatible with observers existing.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Big Bang evidence: expansion + **CMB** + light-element (helium) abundances.
- ★ **Nuclei form first**, atoms at recombination (~380,000 yr) -> CMB.
- ★ Inflation solves **flatness** + **horizon** problems.
- ★ Dark matter = unseen mass; dark energy = accelerating expansion (Type Ia SNe).

notes.title: Ch. 26 — Galaxies
notes.body:
# Galaxies

## Galaxy types
- **Spiral:** disk + arms + gas/dust + star formation.
- **Elliptical:** rounded, old stars, little gas; stars on random orbits (no ordered rotation).
- **Irregular:** no regular shape, often gas-rich and star-forming.
- **Dwarf ellipticals** are the most common (but faint, so long overlooked).

## Discovering other galaxies
- Looked like fuzzy 'nebulae'; **Hubble's Cepheids in Andromeda (1920s)** proved they're far outside the Milky Way (Hubble's first distance was too small).

## Hubble's law -> expansion
- **v = H0 x d:** recession speed ∝ distance -> the universe is **expanding** (space stretches).
- Bound systems (your room, solar system, galaxies themselves) do NOT expand.
- All observers see all galaxies receding -> **no center**.

## Measuring galaxies
- **Mass-to-light ratio:** high in ellipticals / dark-matter-dominated systems; low in star-forming spirals.
- **Distances:** Cepheids + Tully-Fisher (near); **Type Ia supernovae** + Tully-Fisher (far).
- Edge-on disk looks dark due to **dust**.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Spiral / elliptical / irregular; dwarf ellipticals most common.
- ★ **Hubble's law (v = H0 d)** -> expanding universe; space stretches, bound systems don't.
- ★ No center to the expansion.
- ★ Highest mass-to-light ratio -> **dark matter**.

notes.title: Ch. 25 — The Milky Way Galaxy
notes.body:
# The Milky Way Galaxy

## Why a band across the sky
- We sit **inside the flat disk**, so looking along it we see countless stars merged into a hazy band.

## Structure
- **Disk** (spiral arms, gas/dust, young Pop I stars) + **bulge** (old stars) + **halo** (globular clusters, old Pop II stars).
- Embedded in a huge **dark-matter halo** (inferred from the flat **rotation curve**).
- **Nucleus:** supermassive black hole, **Sagittarius A*** (~4 million Msun).

## Where things live
- Globular clusters -> **halo**; molecular clouds + atomic H -> **disk/spiral arms**.

## Populations
- **Pop I:** young, metal-rich, disk, circular orbits (Sun).
- **Pop II:** old, metal-poor, halo/bulge, random orbits.
- Metallicity correlates with location (disk enriched over time; halo formed early).

## Future
- Star formation slows; **collision/merger with Andromeda** in a few billion years.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ We see a band because we're inside the disk.
- ★ Globulars/old Pop II -> halo; young Pop I + star formation -> disk/arms.
- ★ Center: **Sagittarius A***, ~4 million Msun black hole (from stellar orbits).
- ★ Flat rotation curve -> dark matter.

notes.title: Ch. 24 — Black Holes and Curved Spacetime
notes.body:
# Black Holes and Curved Spacetime

## General relativity
- Gravity = **curvature of spacetime** by mass/energy.
- **Equivalence principle:** gravity and acceleration are locally identical -> light bends in gravity -> spacetime is curved.
- **Classic tests:** starlight bending (1919 eclipse), Mercury's perihelion precession, gravitational redshift/time dilation (GPS).

## Black holes
- **Event horizon:** boundary of no escape; its size = **Schwarzschild radius** (∝ mass, so double mass -> double radius).
- **Singularity** at the center (infinite density).
- Sun has NO event horizon (not crushed within ~3 km).
- Falling in: **tidal stretching ('spaghettification')**; outside observers see the image freeze/redden at the horizon.

## Finding the invisible
- Detect via gravity: **X-rays** from an **accretion disk**, stars orbiting an unseen companion >~3 Msun, and **gravitational waves**.
- **Gravitational waves:** ripples in spacetime (LIGO); distortions tinier than a nucleus.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ GR: gravity = curved spacetime; equivalence principle -> light bends.
- ★ Event horizon = no escape; **Schwarzschild radius ∝ mass** (2× mass = 2× radius).
- ★ Sun has no event horizon (not within its ~3 km Schwarzschild radius).
- ★ Detect black holes by X-rays, orbiting stars (>~3 Msun unseen mass), gravitational waves.

notes.title: Ch. 23 — The Death of Stars
notes.body:
# The Death of Stars

## Low/intermediate-mass stars -> white dwarf
- Red giant -> sheds outer layers (**planetary nebula**) -> hot **white dwarf** core.
- Supported by **electron degeneracy**; max mass = **Chandrasekhar limit ~1.4 Msun**.

## Massive stars -> supernova -> neutron star / black hole
- Fuse up to an **iron core** (iron fusion absorbs energy) -> **core collapse** -> **Type II supernova**.
- Leaves a **neutron star** (neutron degeneracy) or, if massive enough, a black hole.
- **Pulsar:** spinning magnetized neutron star -> regular pulses (Crab Nebula is powered by its pulsar).

## Two supernova types
- **Type Ia:** white dwarf in a binary exceeds Chandrasekhar limit -> detonates (NO hydrogen lines).
- **Type II:** core collapse of a single massive star (HAS hydrogen lines).

## Related events
- **Nova:** white dwarf flares from accreted H (survives, can recur) — NOT a supernova.
- **Gamma-ray bursts:** long = massive-star collapse to black hole; short = compact-object mergers.
- **SN 1987A:** neutrino burst confirmed core-collapse theory.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ White dwarf (electron degeneracy, ≤1.4 Msun) vs neutron star (neutron degeneracy, ~20 km).
- ★ Type Ia = white dwarf detonation (no H); Type II = massive-star core collapse (has H).
- ★ Pulsar = spinning neutron star; powers the Crab Nebula.
- ★ Nova ≠ supernova (non-destructive, recurring surface flash).

notes.title: Ch. 22 — Stars from Adolescence to Old Age
notes.body:
# Stars from Adolescence to Old Age

## Life on (and off) the main sequence
- Stars spend most of life fusing H on the **main sequence** (why most stars are seen there).
- Core H gone -> inert He core contracts/heats -> **shell burning** -> swells into a **red giant**.
- He ignites (**triple-alpha** -> carbon; needs higher T because He charge is bigger).
- Later **asymptotic giant** (He+H shells); Sun-like stars stop at carbon/oxygen.

## Luminosity depends on size too
- L = 4 pi R² sigma T⁴ -> a huge cool **supergiant** (Betelgeuse) outshines the Sun.

## Clusters = evolution labs
- Same age/composition/distance, differ by **mass**.
- **Main-sequence turnoff** dates the cluster (lower turnoff = older).
- **Open** clusters: young, metal-rich, disk (Pop I, like the Sun). **Globular:** old, metal-poor, halo (Pop II).

## Recycling
- Stars forge heavy elements (**nucleosynthesis**) and return them via winds/nebulae/supernovae -> new stars/planets/you.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Core H gone -> red giant; He fuses via **triple-alpha** -> carbon.
- ★ Most stars on main sequence (longest phase).
- ★ **Turnoff** dates clusters; lower turnoff = older.
- ★ Open = young/metal-rich; globular = old/metal-poor.
- ★ Heavy elements made in stars, recycled into new generations.

notes.title: Ch. 21 — The Birth of Stars and Exoplanets
notes.body:
# The Birth of Stars and Exoplanets

## Star formation
- Stars form in **cold, dense molecular clouds** (low pressure -> gravity wins).
- A core collapses -> heats -> **protostar** + **accretion disk**.
- Contraction continues until core hits ~10 million K -> **hydrogen fusion** -> joins main sequence.
- Best seen in **infrared** (sees through dust).
- **T Tauri** young stars drive jets -> **Herbig-Haro objects**.
- More massive protostars reach the main sequence **faster**.

## Exoplanets
- **Detection:** radial-velocity (Doppler wobble) + **transit** (starlight dip).
- First around a Sun-like star: 51 Pegasi b, **1995**.
- Surprises: **hot Jupiters**, super-Earths, eccentric/packed orbits -> **planetary migration** added to theory.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Stars form in cold molecular clouds; protostar -> fusion at ~10 million K -> main sequence.
- ★ Massive protostars evolve faster.
- ★ Exoplanet methods: radial-velocity (wobble) + transit (dip).
- ★ Hot Jupiters -> evidence for planetary migration.

notes.title: Ch. 19 — Celestial Distances
notes.body:
# Celestial Distances

## The distance ladder (overlapping methods)
- **Radar ranging:** nearby solar-system bodies (time a reflected signal).
- **Parallax:** nearby stars — Earth's orbit = 1-AU baseline; half the annual shift = parallax angle; trig -> distance.
  - **Parsec:** distance for 1 arcsecond of parallax ≈ 3.26 ly.
  - Limit: tiny shifts become unmeasurable -> only relatively near stars.
- **Standard candles:** objects of known luminosity; compare to apparent brightness (inverse-square law) -> distance.
  - **Cepheids:** period -> luminosity (**period-luminosity relation**); reach nearby galaxies.
  - **RR Lyrae:** standard candles in old populations.
- **Spectroscopic parallax:** spectral type + luminosity class -> luminosity -> distance.
- **Type Ia supernovae:** very luminous standard candles for the most distant galaxies.

## Why a "ladder"?
- Each method is calibrated by the one below it; no single tool spans all distances.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Parallax: 1-AU baseline; **1 parsec = 3.26 ly**; only works nearby.
- ★ Standard candle = known luminosity -> distance via inverse-square law.
- ★ Cepheids: **period -> luminosity** (period-luminosity relation).
- ★ Method by target: radar (solar system), parallax (near stars), Cepheids (near galaxies), Type Ia SNe (far).

notes.title: Ch. 18 — The Stars: A Celestial Census
notes.body:
# The Stars: A Celestial Census

## The H-R diagram (the key tool)
- Axes: **luminosity** (up) vs **surface temperature** (increasing LEFT).
- **Main sequence:** diagonal band, hydrogen fusion — where most stars live (incl. Sun).
- **Giants/supergiants:** upper region (luminous).
- **White dwarfs:** lower left (hot but faint, small remnants).
- **Luminosity class** (I supergiant -> V dwarf) read from the spectrum.

## Measuring masses: binary stars
- Half of stars are in pairs orbiting a **center of mass**.
- **Visual** (resolved), **spectroscopic** (Doppler shifts), **eclipsing** (brightness dips).
- Binaries give **masses**; eclipsing binaries also give **radii** and temperatures.

## Patterns
- **Mass-luminosity relation:** more massive main-sequence stars are far more luminous.
- Up the main sequence (toward upper-left): more mass, more luminosity, hotter, larger.
- Typical galactic star = a **red dwarf** (small, cool, faint, most common).

## Ranges
- Mass ~0.08 to 100+ Msun; luminosity ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁶ Lsun; temp ~2,000 to ~50,000 K; size white-dwarf (Earth-size) to supergiant (~1000× Sun).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ H-R diagram: luminosity vs temperature; main sequence = H fusion.
- ★ White dwarfs = lower-left (hot, faint); supergiants = top.
- ★ **Binaries give masses**; eclipsing binaries also give sizes.
- ★ Mass-luminosity: massive = much more luminous.
- ★ Most common star = red dwarf.

notes.title: Ch. 17 — Analyzing Starlight
notes.body:
# Analyzing Starlight

## Brightness vs luminosity
- **Luminosity:** true energy output per second (intrinsic).
- **Apparent brightness:** how bright it looks here — depends on luminosity AND distance (falls as 1/distance²).
- **Magnitudes:** smaller/negative = brighter (backwards scale). **Absolute magnitude** = brightness at 10 pc (a luminosity measure).

## Color = temperature
- Stars radiate ~blackbody -> peak wavelength sets color (Wien's law). Blue = hot, red = cool.
- **Color index** (brightness in two filters) gauges temperature.

## Spectral classification
- **O B A F G K M** = hottest -> coolest ("Oh Be A Fine Guy/Girl, Kiss Me").
- Cooler extensions: L, T, Y (brown dwarfs).

## Why spectra differ
- Mainly **temperature**, not composition: temperature controls ionization/excitation -> which lines appear.
- Stars are mostly H and He (Payne-Gaposchkin's insight).

## Motion from spectra
- **Doppler shift** of lines -> **radial velocity** (blueshift = approaching, redshift = receding).
- Line broadening -> rotation; Zeeman splitting -> magnetic field.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Apparent brightness = luminosity + distance (1/d²).
- ★ Magnitudes: smaller/negative = brighter.
- ★ OBAFGKM = hot -> cool; color reveals temperature.
- ★ Spectra differ mainly by **temperature**; stars are mostly H/He.
- ★ Doppler: blueshift = toward, redshift = away.

notes.title: Ch. 12 — Rings, Moons, and Pluto
notes.body:
# Rings, Moons, and Pluto

## Rings
- Made of **countless ice + rock particles** (dust to boulders), all in orbit.
- All four giant planets have rings; **Saturn's** are the showpiece.
- **Roche limit:** inside this distance, tidal forces shred a large moon → why rings sit close in.
- **Shepherd moons:** gravity confines ring particles into narrow bands.
- Gaps (e.g., **Cassini Division**) cleared by resonances with moons (Mimas).

## Remarkable moons
- **Titan (Saturn):** only moon with a **thick atmosphere**; methane/ethane lakes.
- **Europa (Jupiter):** icy crust over a **subsurface water ocean**.
- **Enceladus (Saturn):** **geysers** of water from a subsurface ocean.
- **Io (Jupiter):** most **volcanically active** body — powered by **tidal heating**.
- **Cryovolcanism:** icy "lava" (water/ammonia/methane) on cold moons.

## Tidal heating (key concept)
- A planet's changing gravity flexes a moon → internal friction → heat.
- Explains Io's volcanism and helps keep Europa/Enceladus oceans liquid.

## Pluto & the Kuiper Belt
- **Pluto:** dwarf planet, thin atmosphere, varied surface; in the **Kuiper Belt**.
- **Charon:** so large relative to Pluto they're nearly a **double system**.

## Why it matters
- **Subsurface oceans** (Europa, Enceladus) = top targets in the **search for life**.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Rings = small ice/rock particles; **Roche limit** explains why they're close in.
- ★ **Shepherd moons** confine rings; Cassini Division = moon resonance.
- ★ **Io** = most volcanic (tidal heating); **Titan** = thick atmosphere + methane lakes.
- ★ **Europa & Enceladus** = subsurface oceans → life-search targets.
- ★ Pluto = **dwarf planet** in the Kuiper Belt; Charon nearly a twin.

notes.title: Ch. 11 — The Giant Planets
notes.body:
# The Giant Planets

## Two sub-types
- **Gas giants:** Jupiter, Saturn — mostly **hydrogen + helium**.
- **Ice giants:** Uranus, Neptune — more "ices" (water, ammonia, methane); blue from **methane**.
- No solid surface; huge, low density (Saturn's density < water).

## Atmospheres in motion
- **Belts (sinking) and zones (rising)** → banded look (Jupiter, Saturn).
- **Great Red Spot:** centuries-old Jovian storm, bigger than Earth.
- **Differential rotation:** different latitudes spin at different rates (they're fluid).
- **Oblateness:** rapid spin → equatorial bulge, polar flattening.

## Interiors & magnetism
- Deep in Jupiter/Saturn: **metallic hydrogen** (conducts electricity).
- Rapid rotation + conducting fluid → very **strong magnetic fields**.

## Internal heat
- **Jupiter, Saturn, Neptune** emit MORE energy than they receive → internal heat (formation + contraction).

## Oddballs
- **Uranus:** axis tipped ~90 deg → rotates "on its side."
- **Neptune:** most distant, fastest winds, deep blue.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Gas giants (J, S) = H/He; ice giants (U, N) = water/ammonia/methane.
- ★ Strong magnetic fields ← **metallic hydrogen** + fast rotation (J, S).
- ★ Saturn's density is **less than water**.
- ★ J, S, N radiate **more heat than they receive** (internal heat).
- ★ Uranus rotates on its side; Great Red Spot = giant Jovian storm.

notes.title: Ch. 10 — Earthlike Planets: Venus and Mars
notes.body:
# Earthlike Planets: Venus and Mars

## Venus — Earth's "evil twin"
- Earth-sized, but a **thick CO₂ atmosphere** → **runaway greenhouse**.
- Surface ~**460 deg C** (hotter than Mercury) and ~**90x** Earth's pressure.
- **Retrograde**, ultra-slow rotation → day longer than its year.
- **Phases of Venus** (Galileo) supported the **heliocentric** model.

## Mars — the cold desert
- Thin CO₂ atmosphere: **< 1%** of Earth's pressure; cold.
- Giant features: **Olympus Mons** (biggest volcano in solar system) and **Valles Marineris** (canyon ~US-wide).
- **Polar caps:** water ice + frozen CO₂ (dry ice); change with seasons.
- **Evidence of past water:** dried riverbeds, outflow channels, lakebed sediments, water-formed minerals, subsurface **permafrost**.
- Strong dust storms + dunes = **aeolian** (wind) activity.

## Why it matters
- Past liquid water → **habitability** question → Mars is a top target in the search for life.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Venus hottest planet → **runaway greenhouse** (thick CO₂), not proximity to Sun.
- ★ Venus: **retrograde**, very slow spin; ~90x pressure; ~460 deg C.
- ★ Mars: thin CO₂ (<1% Earth), Olympus Mons, Valles Marineris, polar caps.
- ★ Mars shows **past liquid water** → key for life search.
- ★ Phases of Venus = evidence for heliocentrism.

notes.title: Ch. 9 — Cratered Worlds: The Moon and Mercury
notes.body:
# Cratered Worlds: The Moon and Mercury

## Impact cratering (the dominant process)
- **Impact crater:** bowl-shaped scar from a high-speed impact; throws out **ejecta** (bright rays).
- Both worlds are **airless** → no erosion/weather → craters survive billions of years.
- **Crater density = relative age:** more craters = older surface.
- **Late heavy bombardment:** early era of intense impacts (made big basins).

## The Moon's two terrains
- **Maria:** dark, smooth, younger **lava** plains (filled large basins).
- **Highlands:** light, old, heavily cratered.
- **Regolith:** loose pulverized rock/dust from repeated impacts.

## Origin of the Moon
- **Giant impact hypothesis:** Mars-sized body hit young Earth → debris formed the Moon.
- Evidence: Moon's small iron core + composition like Earth's mantle.
- Moon is in **synchronous rotation** (same face toward Earth).

## Mercury
- **Airless**, extreme temperature swings, heavily cratered.
- Unusually **large iron core** for its size.
- **Scarps:** long cliffs → planet **cooled and shrank**, wrinkling the crust.

## Polar ice
- Permanently shadowed polar craters on BOTH worlds may trap **water ice / volatiles**.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ More craters = **older** surface (crater density dating).
- ★ Maria = dark young lava plains; highlands = light old cratered terrain.
- ★ Moon's origin = **giant impact hypothesis**.
- ★ Mercury: oversized **iron core**; **scarps** = cooling/contraction.
- ★ Airless worlds preserve craters (no erosion); polar shadows may hide ice.

notes.title: Ch. 8 — Earth as a Planet
notes.body:
# Earth as a Planet

## Interior structure (outside → in)
- **Crust:** thin solid outer layer.
- **Mantle:** thick rocky layer; slowly **convects**.
- **Core:** dense **iron-nickel**; outer core molten, inner core solid.
- Studied via **seismic waves** (they bend/reflect at layer boundaries).

## A dynamic surface
- **Plate tectonics:** rigid plates ride on convecting mantle → earthquakes, volcanoes, mountains.
- **Erosion:** wind/water/ice wear down features.
- These (plus volcanism) explain why Earth has **few craters** vs the Moon.

## Magnetic field
- Generated by the **dynamo effect** — molten conducting iron + rotation + convection.
- Creates the **magnetosphere**, which deflects the solar wind and shields us.

## Atmosphere
- Composition: ~78% **nitrogen**, ~21% **oxygen**.
- Layers: **troposphere** (weather, temp drops with height), stratosphere (holds the **ozone layer** → blocks UV), and higher layers.

## Greenhouse effect
- Sunlight in (visible) → surface warms → emits infrared → greenhouse gases (CO₂, H₂O) trap it.
- Keeps Earth habitable; without it, Earth would be much **colder**. Too much → overheating.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Layers: crust / mantle / core; probed by **seismic waves**.
- ★ Magnetic field = **dynamo effect** (molten iron core) → magnetosphere shields us.
- ★ Greenhouse effect traps **infrared** → warms surface (CO₂, water vapor).
- ★ Atmosphere ≈ N₂ (78%) + O₂ (21%); ozone layer blocks UV.
- ★ Tectonics + erosion erase craters → Earth looks 'young.'

notes.title: Ch. 7 — Other Worlds: An Introduction to the Solar System
notes.body:
# Other Worlds: An Introduction to the Solar System

## Two families of planets
- **Terrestrial (inner):** Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars.
  - Small, **dense**, rocky, solid surfaces, thin/no atmospheres.
- **Giant / jovian (outer):** Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.
  - Large, **low density**, gas- and ice-rich, NO solid surface, many moons + rings.

## Smaller bodies
- **Asteroids:** rocky/metallic; mostly in the **asteroid belt** (Mars–Jupiter).
- **Comets:** icy; grow head + tail near the Sun.
- **Dwarf planets:** round, orbit Sun, but have NOT cleared their orbit (Pluto, Eris, Ceres).
- **Meteoroid → meteor (in atmosphere) → meteorite (lands).**

## Reservoirs of icy bodies
- **Kuiper Belt:** disk just beyond Neptune → short-period comets; Pluto lives here.
- **Oort cloud:** distant spherical shell → long-period comets.

## Reading a planet from its density
- High density → rock + metal (terrestrials).
- Low density → gas + ice (giants).
- **Differentiation:** dense stuff sinks to core, light stuff floats up → layered interior.

## Origin
- Formed from the **solar nebula** (rotating gas + dust) ~**4.6 billion years** ago.
- **Planetesimals** clumped together to build planets.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Terrestrial = small/dense/rocky/inner; Giant = large/low-density/gas/outer.
- ★ Asteroid belt = between **Mars and Jupiter**.
- ★ Pluto = dwarf planet because it hasn't **cleared its orbit**.
- ★ Kuiper Belt (disk, short-period comets) vs Oort cloud (sphere, long-period comets).
- ★ Density → composition; solar system age ≈ 4.6 billion yr.

notes.title: Ch. 16 — The Sun: A Nuclear Powerhouse
notes.body:
# The Sun: A Nuclear Powerhouse

## The energy source = nuclear fusion
- Core fuses **hydrogen -> helium**; a little **mass -> energy** via **E = mc^2**.
- NOT chemical burning (lasts ~1000s of yr) or contraction (~10s of millions yr).
- Sun has shone steadily ~**4.6 billion years** -> only fusion works.

## The proton-proton chain
1. p + p -> deuterium + positron + **neutrino**
2. deuterium + p -> helium-3 + gamma ray
3. helium-3 + helium-3 -> **helium-4** + 2 p
- Net: 4 hydrogen -> 1 helium-4 + energy.

## Conditions required
- **~10 million K**, very high density/pressure (core only) + quantum tunneling.

## Stability: hydrostatic equilibrium
- Inward **gravity** balanced by outward **pressure** -> stable size.

## Neutrinos
- Escape the core in **seconds** (light takes ~10,000s of yr) -> real-time core probe.
- **Solar neutrino problem:** only ~1/3 detected -> resolved by **neutrino oscillation** -> neutrinos have a tiny **mass**.

## Fusion vs. fission
- **Fusion:** join light nuclei (the Sun). **Fission:** split heavy nuclei (reactors).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Sun powered by **fusion** (H -> He), energy from **E = mc^2**.
- ★ p-p chain net: **4 H -> 1 He-4** + energy.
- ★ **Hydrostatic equilibrium** = gravity vs pressure balance.
- ★ Neutrinos escape instantly -> probe the core; oscillation -> they have mass.
- ★ Fusion (light->heavy) vs fission (heavy->light).

notes.title: Ch. 15 — The Sun: A Garden-Variety Star
notes.body:
# The Sun: A Garden-Variety Star

## Layers (atmosphere, inside -> out)
- **Photosphere:** visible surface, ~**5,800 K**.
- **Chromosphere:** thin reddish layer, ~10,000 K.
- **Corona:** outermost, **1-few million K** but very low density.
- Below the surface: **radiative zone** then **convection zone** (-> **granulation**).

## Magnetic activity
- **Sunspots:** cooler (~4,000 K), dark *by contrast*; strong magnetic fields suppress convection.
- **Solar cycle:** ~**11 yr** sunspot cycle; ~**22 yr** full magnetic cycle (polarity flips each 11 yr).
- Driven by a **dynamo** + **differential rotation** (equator spins faster).
- **Zeeman effect** measures magnetic field strength.

## Eruptions & outflow
- **Flares:** sudden magnetic energy release (radiation across spectrum).
- **Prominences:** arches of cooler gas along field lines.
- **CMEs:** huge plasma eruptions into space.
- **Solar wind:** steady particle outflow from the corona.

## Space weather (effects on Earth)
- Auroras, geomagnetic storms.
- Can damage power grids, satellites, disrupt GPS/radio, endanger astronauts.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Layers + temps: photosphere ~5,800 K -> corona ~millions K (corona is HOTTER than surface).
- ★ Sunspots look dark because they are **cooler** (magnetic suppression of convection).
- ★ **11-yr** sunspot cycle, **22-yr** magnetic cycle (polarity reversal).
- ★ Two space-weather sources: steady **solar wind** vs sudden **CMEs**.
- ★ Energy out: core -> radiative zone -> convection zone -> photosphere.

notes.title: Ch. 14 — Cosmic Samples and the Origin of the Solar System
notes.body:
# Cosmic Samples and the Origin of the Solar System

## The nebular theory
- Sun + planets formed ~**4.6 billion years ago** from the **solar nebula** (rotating gas + dust).
- Disk collapses; densest/hottest center -> **Sun**; surrounding disk -> planets.

## Building planets (the sequence)
1. **Condensation:** gas cools -> solid grains.
2. **Accretion:** grains stick -> **planetesimals**.
3. Planetesimals collide/merge -> protoplanets -> planets.

## The frost line (key idea)
- Inside the **frost (snow) line:** too warm, only rock/metal condense -> small rocky **terrestrial** planets.
- Beyond it: ices condense too -> more solid material -> large **giant** planets that grab H/He gas.

## Cosmic samples
- **Primitive meteorites / chondrites** preserve original nebula material (with **chondrules**).
- **Radioactive dating:** parent/daughter isotope ratio + **half-life** -> age since the rock solidified (not necessarily the whole world).

## Exoplanets (testing the theory)
- **Detection:** radial-velocity (star wobble) and **transit** (starlight dips).
- Surprises: **hot Jupiters** (giants very close in), super-Earths, eccentric/packed orbits -> forced revisions to planet-formation models (e.g., planetary migration).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Order: condensation -> accretion -> planetesimals -> planets.
- ★ **Frost line** explains rocky-inner vs gas/ice-outer planets.
- ★ Radiometric dating = parent/daughter + **half-life**; dates the rock, not the planet.
- ★ Common solar-system properties (same direction, plane, age) -> single nebula.
- ★ Exoplanets (hot Jupiters etc.) forced revisions to formation theory.

notes.title: Ch. 13 — Comets and Asteroids: Debris of the Solar System
notes.body:
# Comets and Asteroids: Debris of the Solar System

## Asteroids
- Rocky/metallic leftovers, mostly in the **asteroid belt** (Mars-Jupiter).
- Never formed a planet (Jupiter's gravity prevented it).
- **Trojans** share Jupiter's orbit at stable points; **NEOs** cross near Earth (impact hazard).

## Comets
- Icy 'dirty snowballs' from the cold outer solar system.
- **Three parts when active:** nucleus (km-sized icy core), coma (glowing gas/dust cloud), tails.
- **Two tails, both point AWAY from the Sun:**
  - **Ion tail:** gas pushed straight back by the **solar wind**.
  - **Dust tail:** dust pushed by sunlight, curves along the orbit.
- **Sublimation** (solid -> gas) near the Sun creates the coma/tails.
- **Short-period** comets <- **Kuiper Belt**; **long-period** <- **Oort cloud**.

## Meteoroids, meteors, meteorites
- **Meteoroid:** particle in space.
- **Meteor:** the light streak as it burns up ('shooting star').
- **Meteorite:** what survives and lands.
- **Meteor showers:** Earth crosses a comet's debris stream; meteors appear to radiate from one point (perspective).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Comet tails ALWAYS point **away from the Sun** (ion = straight, dust = curved).
- ★ Comet parts: **nucleus, coma, tail**; activity driven by **sublimation**.
- ★ Short-period comets = **Kuiper Belt**; long-period = **Oort cloud**.
- ★ Asteroid belt = **Mars to Jupiter**; comets icy vs asteroids rocky.
- ★ Meteoroid (space) -> meteor (streak) -> meteorite (lands).

notes.title: Ch. 6 — Astronomical Instruments
notes.body:
# Astronomical Instruments

## What telescopes do
- Collect and focus electromagnetic radiation so faint, distant objects can be studied.
- They do NOT just magnify — their main job is **gathering light** and **resolving detail**.

## Two basic optical designs
- **Refractor:** uses a **lens**. Suffers **chromatic aberration** (colors focus differently); hard to make large.
- **Reflector:** uses a **mirror**. No chromatic aberration; can be made huge. **All large research telescopes are reflectors.**

## Aperture = the key spec
- **Aperture** = diameter of the main lens/mirror.
- **Light-gathering power ∝ diameter²** → double the diameter = **4×** the light.
- Bigger aperture also = **better resolution** (finer detail).

## Detectors
- **CCDs** (electronic) replaced photographic plates — far more efficient and digital.

## Earth's atmosphere = the enemy
- **Atmospheric seeing:** turbulence blurs/twinkles images, limiting ground resolution.
- **Adaptive optics:** mirror flexes rapidly to cancel turbulence → sharp ground images.

## Beyond the ground / beyond visible light
- **Space telescopes:** avoid seeing AND reach blocked wavelengths (UV, X-ray, much IR).
- **Radio telescopes:** big dishes for radio waves (must be large for good resolution).
- **Interferometers:** link separated telescopes → resolution of a dish as wide as their spacing.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ **Aperture** is the most important property (light-gathering + resolution).
- ★ Light-gathering ∝ **diameter²** (2× diameter → 4× light).
- ★ Big telescopes = **reflectors** (mirrors); refractors suffer **chromatic aberration**.
- ★ Space telescopes: escape **seeing** + reach **UV/X-ray/IR** blocked by the atmosphere.
- ★ Interferometry links dishes for the resolution of a much larger instrument.

notes.title: Ch. 5 — Radiation and Spectra
notes.body:
# Radiation and Spectra

## Light = electromagnetic radiation
- Oscillating electric + magnetic fields; travels at **~300,000 km/s** in vacuum.
- Wave AND particle: a particle of light is a **photon**.
- **Wavelength** ↔ **frequency** ↔ **energy**:
  - short wavelength = high frequency = high energy
  - long wavelength = low frequency = low energy

## The electromagnetic spectrum (long → short λ)
- **Radio → microwave → infrared → visible → ultraviolet → X-ray → gamma ray**
- Visible light is a tiny slice in the middle.

## Blackbody / thermal radiation
- A hot dense object radiates a continuous spectrum set only by its **temperature**.
- **Wien's law:** hotter → peaks at **shorter (bluer)** wavelength → color tells temperature.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann law:** total energy radiated rises as **T⁴** (very steep).

## Three spectra (Kirchhoff's laws)
- **Continuous:** hot, **dense** object (solid/dense gas).
- **Emission lines:** hot, **thin** gas → bright lines at specific λ.
- **Absorption lines:** continuous source seen **through** a cooler thin gas → dark lines.
- Lines act like fingerprints → identify **elements**.

## Doppler effect
- Source receding → **redshift** (longer λ).
- Source approaching → **blueshift** (shorter λ).
- Lets us measure motion of stars and galaxies along the line of sight.

## Why it matters
- **Spectroscopy** reveals composition, temperature, density, and motion of objects we can never visit.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ EM spectrum order: radio → micro → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma.
- ★ Shorter λ = higher frequency = higher energy.
- ★ Wien's law: hotter = bluer peak (color → temperature).
- ★ Spectra: dense=continuous, thin hot gas=emission, cool gas in front=absorption.
- ★ Doppler: redshift = receding, blueshift = approaching.

notes.title: Ch. 4 — Earth, Moon, and Sky
notes.body:
# Earth, Moon, and Sky

## Position on Earth
- **Latitude:** degrees N/S of the equator (0–90 deg).
- **Longitude:** degrees E/W of the prime meridian.

## Rotation vs. revolution
- **Rotation:** spin on axis → day/night.
- **Revolution:** orbit the Sun → the year.
- **Sidereal day:** ~23h 56m (vs. stars). **Solar day:** ~24h (vs. Sun) — longer because Earth also moves along its orbit.

## Seasons (cause = AXIAL TILT, not distance)
- Axis tilted **23.5 deg**.
- Hemisphere tilted toward Sun → direct light + long days = **summer**.
- Opposite hemisphere has winter at the same time → distance can't be the cause.
- **Solstices:** longest/shortest days (Sun farthest from celestial equator).
- **Equinoxes:** ~equal day/night (Sun on celestial equator).

## The Moon
- Shines by **reflected sunlight**; phases come from Sun-Earth-Moon geometry.
- **Synchronous rotation:** spins once per orbit → same face always toward Earth.
- New moon (between us & Sun) → full moon (opposite Sun) and back, ~29.5 days.

## Eclipses
- **Solar:** Moon between Sun and Earth (at new moon).
- **Lunar:** Earth between Sun and Moon (at full moon).
- Not monthly because the Moon's orbit is tilted ~5 deg → alignment is usually off.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Seasons = **axial tilt (23.5 deg)**, NOT Earth–Sun distance.
- ★ Same face of Moon → **synchronous rotation** (spins once per orbit).
- ★ Solar eclipse: **Moon in the middle**; lunar eclipse: **Earth in the middle**.
- ★ No monthly eclipses because the Moon's orbit is tilted ~5 deg.
- ★ Solstice = longest/shortest day; equinox = equal day/night.

notes.title: Ch. 3 — Orbits and Gravity
notes.body:
# Orbits and Gravity

## Kepler's three laws (empirical, from Tycho's data)
- **1st (Law of Ellipses):** Planets orbit the Sun in **ellipses**, Sun at one **focus**.
- **2nd (Law of Equal Areas):** The planet-Sun line sweeps **equal areas in equal times** → planet is **fastest at perihelion**, slowest at aphelion.
- **3rd (Harmonic Law):** **P^2 = a^3** (P in years, a in AU). Bigger orbit → much longer period.

## Ellipse vocabulary
- **Foci:** two defining points; Sun sits at one.
- **Eccentricity (e):** 0 = circle, near 1 = very stretched.
- **Semimajor axis (a):** half the long axis = average distance from Sun.
- **Perihelion:** closest to Sun. **Aphelion:** farthest.

## Newton's laws of motion
- **1st:** inertia — no net force → no change in motion.
- **2nd:** **F = ma**.
- **3rd:** equal and opposite reaction; forces come in pairs.

## Universal gravitation
- Every mass attracts every other mass.
- Force ∝ (m1 × m2) and ∝ 1/distance² → **inverse-square law**.
- Double the distance → force drops to **1/4**.

## Why orbits happen
- Inertia wants straight-line motion; gravity pulls toward the central body.
- The combination = continuous 'falling around' → a closed orbit.
- **Escape velocity:** minimum speed to leave for good.

## Newton unifies it
- Kepler's laws are *derived* from Newton's motion laws + inverse-square gravity.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Memorize **P^2 = a^3** (years, AU). a=4 → P=8 yr.
- ★ Fastest at **perihelion** (2nd law / equal areas).
- ★ Gravity is **inverse-square**: 2× distance → 1/4 force.
- ★ Orbits = inertia + gravity (falling around the Sun).
- ★ Newton's 2nd law: **F = ma**.

notes.title: Ch. 2 — Observing the Sky: The Birth of Astronomy
notes.body:
# Observing the Sky: The Birth of Astronomy

## The celestial sphere (a model)
- An imaginary sphere with the stars 'attached'; useful for describing positions.
- **Celestial poles:** Earth's axis extended; sky appears to spin around them.
- **Celestial equator:** projection of Earth's equator.
- **Zenith:** point directly overhead. **Horizon:** where sky meets ground.

## Apparent motions
- **Daily:** sky rises in east, sets in west — caused by Earth's **rotation**.
- **Yearly:** Sun drifts eastward through the **zodiac** along the **ecliptic** — caused by Earth's **orbit**.
- The ecliptic is also the plane of Earth's orbit.

## Brightness: the magnitude system
- Smaller number = brighter; the brightest objects have negative magnitudes.
- It is a backwards/counterintuitive scale (inherited from the Greeks).

## Ancient models
- **Geocentric (Earth-centered):** Aristotle/Ptolemy. Used **epicycles** to fake retrograde motion.
- **Heliocentric (Sun-centered):** Copernicus (1500s). Retrograde motion falls out naturally from Earth overtaking outer planets.

## Retrograde motion
- A planet appears to drift backward (westward) for weeks, then resumes eastward motion.
- Heliocentric explanation: changing line of sight as Earth laps an outer planet — no real reversal.

## Precession
- Slow ~26,000-year conical wobble of Earth's axis.
- Changes which star is the 'pole star' over millennia (e.g., not always Polaris).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Daily sky motion = Earth's **rotation**; yearly Sun motion = Earth's **orbit**.
- ★ Ecliptic = Sun's apparent yearly path = plane of Earth's orbit.
- ★ Geocentric used **epicycles**; heliocentric (Copernicus) explains retrograde naturally.
- ★ Magnitudes: **smaller/negative = brighter**.
- ★ Precession ≈ 26,000-year axial wobble; changes the pole star.

notes.title: Ch. 1 — Science and the Universe: A Brief Tour
notes.body:
# Science and the Universe: A Brief Tour

## What astronomy is
- Astronomy is the study of everything beyond Earth and how those objects interact.
- It is also the effort to assemble what we learn into a single history of the universe, from the Big Bang to now.
- Science is a *progress report*: ideas change as new instruments and techniques let us probe deeper.

## A sense of scale (key distances)
- **Astronomical unit (AU):** Earth–Sun distance, ~150 million km. Used inside the solar system.
- **Light-year:** distance light travels in one year, ~9.5 trillion km. Used between stars and galaxies. (It is a *distance*, not a time.)
- Light speed is finite, so distance = looking back in time.

## Structure of the universe (small to large)
- Stars (like the Sun)
- Solar system (Sun + planets, moons, asteroids, comets)
- Galaxy (the Milky Way: a few hundred billion stars)
- Groups/clusters of galaxies
- The universe as a whole

## Time
- The universe is about **13.8 billion years** old (since the Big Bang).
- Earth and the solar system are about **4.6 billion years** old.

## How science works
- **Hypothesis:** a testable tentative explanation.
- **Theory:** a broad, repeatedly tested explanation backed by lots of evidence (a *strong* word in science).
- **Law of nature:** a concise, often mathematical statement of a regularity.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ A light-year is a unit of **distance**, not time.
- ★ One AU ≈ 150 million km (Earth–Sun distance).
- ★ Universe age ≈ 13.8 billion years; solar system/Earth ≈ 4.6 billion years.
- ★ Finite light speed → distant = past ("telescopes are time machines").
- ★ In science, a "theory" is well-supported, not a mere guess.
